summary: Multi-agent professional/enterprise design review of sentrize.com from captured assets

result.overall_verdict: This is not a palette — it is three unrelated theme colors bolted onto a lifted Verifone.com template, plus a black/white/gray substrate that switches on almost every section. Pixel-sampling the screenshots confirms accent hues spanning a 63-degree arc across THREE distinct families: emerald green (H158-159, the hero gradient #03c37e-#04c783), cyan/teal (H188-192, #22d3ee and #0891b2), and electric blue (H210-221, #0a84ff / #004aef / #3474ff). No single hue is used consistently enough to register as "the brand color." A confident enterprise brand commits to one accent (Stripe purple, Vercel black, Datadog purple) and repeats it everywhere; here the hero says green, the mid-page hero-block says cyan, the buttons and logo say blue, and the footer goes navy. The result reads exactly as reported — AI-assembled and non-enterprise — because the color system has no author. It has the fingerprints of a template whose original blue theme (Verifone) was partially overpainted with teal (theme-teal.css) and a green hero, none of which were reconciled. Compounding this, several color pairings fail WCAG contrast, which no shipped enterprise design system would allow.

findings.title: Three competing accent hue families in one page (green vs cyan/teal vs blue), spanning a 63-degree hue arc
findings.evidence: Pixel-sampled from the screenshots and design-tokens.json, the accents cluster into three unrelated families by HSL hue: (1) EMERALD GREEN hero gradient #03c37e / #04c783 (H158-159, S~97%); (2) CYAN/TEAL blocks #22d3ee (H188, the 'Built for ambitious technology teams' block and CTA band) and #0891b2 (H192, used as both background x3 and text-color x7); (3) BLUE #0a84ff (H210, skip-link), #004aef (H221, logo/nav region) and #3474ff (H221, body link text). Hue spread = 158 to 221 degrees. design-tokens.json lists #0891b2, #22d3ee, #0a84ff, #004aef AND #3474ff as separate committed values — the green gradient does not even appear in the token list because it is an ad-hoc CSS gradient, meaning it is a fourth un-tokenized accent.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Real brands own ONE accent hue. Three saturated primaries with no shared relationship is the visual signature of a lifted template (Verifone's blue) partially re-skinned with a teal theme file (theme-teal.css) and a green hero, with nobody reconciling them. It looks generated because each section was evidently themed in isolation — the exact 'plausible in isolation, incoherent in aggregate' pattern of AI/template assembly.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE accent family and delete the other two from the codebase. Given the 'engineering/cloud' positioning and the teal-named CSS, commit to a single teal — e.g. #0891b2 as the primary with #22d3ee reserved only as a hover/active tint of that same hue — and remove the green hero gradient and all three blues (#0a84ff, #004aef, #3474ff) entirely. Replace every blue button/link with the chosen teal. Enforce it as CSS custom properties (--accent, --accent-hover) so no section can drift.

findings.title: Section-background whiplash: 9 background changes cascading dark to light to dark to color
findings.evidence: A top-to-bottom pixel scan of desktop-full.png (6215px tall) shows the background flips through: #000000 black (nav, 0-3%) -> #04c07e green hero (6-9%) -> #ffffff white (12%) -> #ececec light gray (21%) -> #000000 black (27%) -> #1a1a1a near-black (33%) -> #ffffff / #f5f5f5 (39-48%) -> #22d3ee cyan block (51%) -> #000000 (57%) -> #333333 gray (60%) -> #e6e6e6 gray (75-82%) -> #22d3ee cyan CTA (87%) -> #1c1c26 navy footer (93%). That is roughly nine distinct background regimes, alternating light and dark with two full-bleed saturated-cyan interruptions. The backgrounds token histogram confirms the fragmentation: #ffffff(11), #000000(8), #e6e6e6(6), #ececec(5), #1a1a1a(4), #333333(4), #0891b2(3), #22d3ee(3), plus #0a84ff, #004aef, #f5f5f5, #1c1c26.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Enterprise pages use a restrained rhythm — typically 2 to 3 background tones (e.g. white, one light gray, one dark) alternating predictably. Nine regimes with two neon-cyan full-bleed bands feels like a stack of unrelated template sections pasted together, which is precisely how a lifted/AI-assembled page is built. The eye never settles, and the two cyan bands read as 'demo blocks' rather than an intentional brand moment.
findings.recommendation: Collapse to a 3-tone system: white (#ffffff) as default, ONE neutral (pick #f5f5f5 OR #ececec OR #e6e6e6 — currently all three exist), and ONE dark (pick #000000 OR #1a1a1a OR #1c1c26 — currently all three exist). Use the single accent (teal) only for small elements, never as a full-bleed section background; convert the two #22d3ee bands to white/dark with teal accents. Establish a fixed alternation (e.g. white, dark, white) so section transitions feel authored.

findings.title: Redundant near-duplicate neutrals reveal no design-token discipline
findings.evidence: The palette carries THREE light grays that are visually near-identical — #e6e6e6, #ececec, #f5f5f5 — used as separate section backgrounds, and THREE dark tones — #000000, #1a1a1a, #1c1c26 — used interchangeably for dark sections/footer. On the text side there are four low-chroma grays doing the same job: #5b5b6b (49 uses), #9a9aad (31), #c7c9d4 (7), #82829a (2). #1c1c26 and #82829a/#9a9aad carry a faint purple cast (H240 / desaturated) that does not match the neutral #333333 grays elsewhere, so even the 'neutrals' are from two different source systems.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A hand-built design system has ONE gray ramp with deliberate steps. Three interchangeable light grays and three interchangeable darks is what you get when multiple template fragments (each with its own gray) are concatenated without a token pass. The mixed-temperature grays (cool-purple #9a9aad vs neutral #333333) are a tell that colors came from different origins — a hallmark of stitched-together / AI output.
findings.recommendation: Define one neutral ramp of ~5 steps (e.g. #ffffff, #f5f5f5, #6b7280 text-muted, #333333, #0d0d0d) and map every existing near-duplicate onto it. Kill #e6e6e6+#ececec (keep one), kill #1a1a1a+#1c1c26 (keep #000 or one dark), and standardize body text on a single muted gray. Remove the purple-tinted grays so the neutrals stay temperature-consistent.

findings.title: White text on the #22d3ee cyan blocks fails WCAG contrast (1.81:1)
findings.evidence: The full-bleed #22d3ee cyan blocks (mid-page 'Built for ambitious technology teams' at y~51%, and the CTA 'Ready to build your next product with confidence?' band at y~87%) carry white text. White (#ffffff) on #22d3ee measures 1.81:1 — far below the 4.5:1 AA threshold and even below the 3:1 large-text floor. Black on that same cyan would be 11.62:1. The design-tokens buttons also show a 'Skip to content' control at #0a84ff which is fine, but the cyan-band body copy is the problem.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Shipping white-on-light-cyan is a mistake a mature design system's contrast linting would never pass. It signals the cyan was dropped in as a decorative template block without checking legibility — an amateur/automated move. It also visually 'glows' and vibrates, reinforcing the cheap feel.
findings.recommendation: On any #22d3ee surface, switch text to near-black (#0d0d0d gives 11.6:1). Better, per finding #1, stop using #22d3ee as a full-bleed background entirely; if a colored CTA band is wanted, use a dark surface with teal accents, which keeps white text legible and looks more enterprise.

findings.title: Muted body/label text below contrast minimums (#9a9aad at 2.76:1)
findings.evidence: #9a9aad is the second-most-used text color (31 instances) and on white measures only 2.76:1 — fails AA (4.5:1) and fails even the 3:1 large-text bar. Additionally #0891b2 used as text on white (7 instances) is 3.68:1 (large-text only, fails for body), and body link #3474ff on white is 4.13:1 (just short of 4.5:1 AA). The primary body gray #5b5b6b (49 uses) is fine at 6.66:1, so the failures are the secondary/muted tiers.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Using a too-light gray for secondary copy is a classic 'looks elegant in the mockup, illegible in production' error that automated/template layouts inherit. Enterprise sites tune muted text to clear AA. The cyan and blue used AS text (rather than as fills) at sub-4.5:1 is another sign the accent colors were chosen for looks, not tested for function.
findings.recommendation: Darken muted text to at least #6b6b7a (~4.6:1) or preferably a single muted token around #595966. Never use #0891b2 or #3474ff as small/body text on white — reserve accent hues for fills, icons, and large headings only, where they clear the 3:1 large-text bar.

findings.title: The hero alone collides two of the three accent families, undercutting first impression
findings.evidence: Sweeping the hero gradient left-to-right at mid-height: it starts emerald #03c37e (H158) on the left, and by ~20% across the sampled band the pixels read #0891b2 (H192) — the gradient literally blends green into cyan within the same hero panel. Simultaneously the nav/logo region samples #004aef electric blue (H221). So within the single first viewport the visitor sees green, cyan, AND blue at once, over an Unsplash stock photo of a person at a laptop.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The hero is the one place a brand must state its color identity unambiguously. A green-to-cyan gradient sitting next to a blue logo says the brand has not decided what color it is. Combined with stock photography, it reads as a generic AI/template hero rather than a considered enterprise brand statement.
findings.recommendation: Make the hero monochromatic in the chosen accent: a single-hue teal gradient (e.g. #0891b2 to a darker #075e78) or a solid dark surface with one teal accent. Recolor the logo/nav to the same family so nothing in the first viewport contradicts the brand hue. Replace the stock hero image or desaturate/overlay it in-brand so the photo does not introduce yet another color temperature.

result.overall_verdict: This type system reads unmistakably as a lifted template with the labels filed off, not an original enterprise brand. The single most damning fact is verifiable in design-tokens.json: the entire site renders in Verifone-Sans (Verifone-Sans-Regular alone is used 550 times, plus -Medium/-SemiBold/-Bold and a "Verifone Sans" stack) — the proprietary corporate typeface of Verifone, a publicly-traded payments company. An agency shipping a competitor's licensed brand font is both a legal/licensing liability and a dead giveaway that the page is a reskinned Verifone.com template. On top of that, every single heading (all 7, from the 52px H1 down) is set at font-weight 300 (light), so the page has no typographic "voice" — the biggest words on the screen are the thinnest, which is the opposite of how confident enterprise brands set display type. The size scale is a tell too: the four fractional sizes (13.12, 15.36, 15.2, 14.4px) are rem-to-pixel conversions inherited wholesale from the source template rather than chosen, and the overall ramp (13.12→14→15.2→15.36→16→20→24→28→30→32→44→52) has ~9 clustered small sizes doing barely-distinguishable jobs. Finally, headings carry literal hard line breaks (\n plus indentation) baked into the text, a hand-tuning hack that breaks the moment the viewport changes. The net effect is thin, generic, and templated — exactly the "AI-generated, non-enterprise" impression the owner was warned about.

findings.title: Entire site is set in Verifone-Sans — a payments company's proprietary, licensed brand font
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json fontFamilies: "Verifone-Sans-Regular, sans-serif" (550 occurrences), "\"Verifone Sans\", Verifone-Sans-Regular, system-ui..." (23), "Verifone-Sans-Medium, sans-serif" (7), "Verifone-Sans-SemiBold" (2), "Verifone-Sans-Bold" (1). Verifone-Sans is the corporate typeface of Verifone (the payment-terminal company); it is not a Google/open font. This matches the already-noted lifted assets (chrome.css/enterprise.css, sh__nav classes).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Shipping another (competitor-adjacent) corporation's proprietary brand font is the single clearest proof the page is a reskinned Verifone.com template rather than an original design. Verifone-Sans is not licensed for third-party redistribution, so it will 404 for many visitors and silently fall back to plain sans-serif — meaning the 'brand' typeface isn't even reliably rendering. A real engineering agency owns its type choice; borrowing a payments firm's font is an amateur/AI-assembly signature and a licensing exposure.
findings.recommendation: Rip out every Verifone-Sans reference and self-host a properly-licensed typeface that signals engineering credibility — e.g. Inter, IBM Plex Sans, or a geometric grotesque like Söhne/Neue Montreal (paid) — with real weight files (400/500/600/700). Replace the font-family stacks in the CSS with a single named token (e.g. --font-brand) and delete the Verifone fallbacks so the intended font actually loads for all users.

findings.title: All headings are font-weight 300 (light), including the 52px H1 — no weight hierarchy anywhere
findings.evidence: In design-tokens.json every heading object has "weight": "300": H1 52px/300 ("We build the software..."), the three 44px H2s/300 ("Choose the engagement model...", "Why teams keep building with us", "Power your roadmap..."), H3 32px/300, and the 30px CTA H2/300. The rendered hero (desktop-hero.png) and the 'Choose the engagement model' / 'Built for ambitious technology teams' headings visibly show hairline strokes at large sizes.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Weight 300 is a display weight for editorial/fashion brands, not for a B2B engineering agency that wants to look solid and authoritative. Setting the single largest element on the page (52px H1) in the thinnest available weight makes the headline feel fragile and generic, and it collapses the primary contrast dimension typography has: there is no bold anchor anywhere, so nothing feels load-bearing. This uniform thinness is a classic default-template / AI-generated look.
findings.recommendation: Establish real weight contrast: set H1/H2 display headings to 600–700 (SemiBold/Bold), sub-heads to 500–600, and reserve 300/400 only for large lead paragraphs if desired. Keep letter-spacing slightly negative (-0.01 to -0.02em) on 44px+ headings so the heavier weight stays refined. The hero H1 in particular should be the boldest, most confident type on the page.

findings.title: The hero statistics (500+, 120+, 99.99%) render in light weight — the numbers meant to impress look weak
findings.evidence: In the stats band (desktop-full.png ~y3780) '500+' above 'PROJECTS DELIVERED', and '99.99%' above 'Enterprise reliability', are rendered with visibly thin strokes at large size, consistent with the site-wide weight-300 heading treatment. These are the trust-building claims (500+ projects, 120+ engineers, 99.99% uptime, 40+ countries).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Big-number stat blocks are supposed to hit hard — they are the visual proof of scale. Setting them in a hairline weight undercuts the exact message ('we are big and reliable') they exist to convey, so the section reads as decorative filler rather than evidence. Combined with the unverifiable-looking round numbers, thin figures make the claims feel even more like placeholder AI copy.
findings.recommendation: Set the stat figures at 700+ weight (or a dedicated tabular/display cut), enlarge them relative to their labels, and give the uppercase labels (e.g. 'PROJECTS DELIVERED') a consistent smaller size with measured letter-spacing. Numbers are the one place on this page that must feel heavy.

findings.title: Fractional font sizes (13.12, 15.36, 15.2, 14.4px) are inherited rem conversions, not deliberate choices
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json fontSizes includes "13.12px" (53 uses — the second-most-common size on the page), "15.36px" (7), "15.2px" (2), "14.4px" (1). These map to 0.82rem (13.12), 0.96rem (15.36), 0.95rem (15.2) and 0.9rem (14.4) at a 16px root — values carried over verbatim from the Verifone template's rem-based scale. The nav buttons use 15.36px, the mega-menu items 15.2px.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: No designer hand-picks 13.12px or 15.36px; these oddities only appear when rem values from someone else's system are computed against a 16px root. Their presence is a fingerprint that the type scale was inherited rather than authored. Using 13.12px for 53 elements (the workhorse body/UI size) means most of the page's text sits at a non-round, template-derived size.
findings.recommendation: Define a clean, intentional scale in round px or a consistent rem step and apply it everywhere: e.g. 13 / 14 / 16 / 18 / 20 / 24 / 32 / 44 / 56. Collapse 13.12→13 or 14 and 15.2/15.36→15 or 16. Publish it as design tokens so no stray computed values survive.

findings.title: Nine clustered small/mid sizes doing overlapping jobs — the scale lacks coherent steps
findings.evidence: The full size ramp in design-tokens.json is 13.12, 14, 14.4, 15.2, 15.36, 16, 20, 24, 28, 30, 32, 44, 52. Between 13 and 16px there are five nearly-indistinguishable sizes (13.12/14/14.4/15.2/15.36/16), and two near-duplicate headline tiers (44px H2 vs 52px H1, and 30px vs 32px). The 30px CTA H2 ('Ready to build your next product with confidence?') and the 32px H3 ('Beyond delivery...') are only 2px apart yet are different heading levels.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A coherent type system has a small number of clearly-separated steps (typically a ~1.2–1.25 ratio). Here the mid-range is a smear of six sizes within 3px of each other, so the reader gets no clear sense of hierarchy — everything is 'medium'. Near-duplicate tiers (30 vs 32, 44 vs 52) signal sizes accreted from multiple copied components rather than one designed ramp, a hallmark of stitched-together/AI output.
findings.recommendation: Consolidate to ~6 roles: caption 13, body 16, lead 20, H3 28, H2 36, H1 52 (adjust to taste but keep clear gaps). Merge 13.12/14/14.4/15.2/15.36 into at most two body sizes; merge the 30px CTA heading into the 32px H3 tier or push it up so it reads as a distinct level.

findings.title: Headings contain hard-coded manual line breaks baked into the copy
findings.evidence:
design-tokens.json headings show literal newlines and indentation inside the text: H1 = "We build the software
                    
                    and cloud platfor..."; H2 = "Choose the engagement model
        
        that fits your team"; H2 = "Power your roadmap with the
        
        Sentrize engineering team". The whitespace runs indicate the breaks were typed into the markup/JSX rather than controlled by CSS.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai:
Hand-inserting
/newlines to force a headline to wrap a certain way is a fragile hack: it only looks right at one viewport width and breaks (orphans, awkward mid-phrase wraps) at every other size. Enterprise design systems control wrapping with max-width/measure and balanced-wrap, not literal line breaks. Baked-in breaks with trailing indentation whitespace are also a tell of copy pasted into a template by hand or by an AI.
findings.recommendation: Remove all manual line breaks from heading text. Constrain heading width with a max-width (e.g. ~18–24ch for the H1) and use text-wrap: balance (with a pretty/balance fallback) so wrapping adapts responsively. Let the layout, not hard breaks, decide the line count.

findings.title: Body and muted text lean on two low-contrast greys that read thin and washed-out
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json textColors: the most-used text color is "#5b5b6b" (49 uses) and "#9a9aad" (31 uses), with "#c7c9d4" (7) and "#82829a" (2). Much of this sits on near-black backgrounds (#000000, #1a1a1a, #1c1c26). In the crops, supporting copy like 'Production systems shipped across 40+ countries' and 'SLA-backed uptime with 24/7 monitoring' renders in a dim grey.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: #5b5b6b on #1a1a1a is very low contrast (well under WCAG AA for body text), so paragraphs feel faint and the light-weight font compounds the thinness. Defaulting large amounts of copy to muted grey-on-dark is a common template/AI aesthetic that prioritizes 'sleek' over legibility and makes the page feel insubstantial rather than authoritative.
findings.recommendation: Raise body text on dark sections to at least #c7c9d4 / #d4d6e0 (≥4.5:1) and reserve #5b5b6b/#9a9aad only for true de-emphasized captions. Pair the contrast bump with a heavier body weight (400 minimum) so paragraphs read solid. Verify AA contrast for every text/background pair.

findings.title: Letter-spaced uppercase eyebrows and wordmark are Verifone-template mannerisms, not an owned style
findings.evidence: The 'SENTRIZE' wordmark (nav and footer, desktop-hero.png / footer crop) is set as tracked-out uppercase, and section eyebrows like 'FLEXIBLE WAYS TO WORK WITH US' (above the 44px H2) and 'PROJECTS DELIVERED' use the same small letter-spaced caps treatment seen throughout Verifone's marketing template.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The tracked-caps eyebrow over a thin light-weight headline is the exact rhythm of the source Verifone layout. Reusing that mannerism while also carrying the Verifone font makes the brand feel like a costume rather than an identity — recognizable to anyone who has seen the original template, and generic to everyone else.
findings.recommendation: Once the font is replaced, define eyebrow style intentionally (e.g. 12–13px, 500–600 weight, +0.08em tracking, in the teal accent #0891b2 for brand tie-in) and give the wordmark a deliberate, spacing/weight treatment distinct from the body font so the identity feels authored rather than inherited.

result.overall_verdict: This reads as a bolted-together lift of the Verifone.com template, not a cohesive original design. The strongest tells are systemic, not cosmetic: two competing accent-color systems coexist on the same page (a blue #0a84ff/gradient logo system inherited from the source template vs. a cyan #22d3ee/#0891b2 system layered on top), so the identical button label "Start a project" renders BLUE in the header and CYAN in the hero — a mistake no in-house design team makes. The section rhythm is a mechanical A/B/A/B stack of full-bleed white → black → white → cyan bands with no connective tissue, and each band uses a different card treatment (pure-black #000 cards, dark-gray #1a1a1a cards, light-gray #e6e6e6 cards) and a different corner radius, so the eye reads it as several templates spliced together. The design-token export confirms zero radius discipline (11 distinct corner values). The icon set is the giveaway detail: the engagement-model and benefit cards are illustrated with payment-terminal / POS card-reader glyphs from the Verifone product library, which have nothing to do with a software-engineering agency. Net: it looks amateur/AI-assembled because the parts were never reconciled into one system.

findings.title: Two competing accent-color systems — identical 'Start a project' button is blue in the header, cyan in the hero
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json buttons array shows the header 'Skip to content'/CTA using bg #0a84ff, and the site logo (header + footer) is a blue→purple gradient S-mark. But the hero 'Start a project' button, the 'Most popular' badge, the active 'SaaS & Software' pill, the 'Built for ambitious technology teams' block, and the final CTA band are all cyan/teal (backgrounds list #22d3ee ×3, #0891b2 ×3; textColors list #0891b2 ×7). The verbatim label 'Start a project' appears twice: header crop renders it solid BLUE (#0a84ff), hero crop renders it CYAN (#0891b2). Same word, two colors, one viewport.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A single brand ships one primary accent. Having two — where the leftover blue is clearly the Verifone source-template color and the cyan is the new paint job — proves the theme was re-skinned by find-replace without unifying the button/logo tokens. The duplicated-label color split is the single most obvious 'this is a lifted template' tell on the page.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE accent. Since the body is cyan, recolor the logo, header CTA (#0a84ff → #0891b2), and 'Skip to content' link to the cyan ramp, or vice-versa. Delete #0a84ff, #004aef, #3474ff from the palette entirely. There should be exactly one 'primary button' style, not a blue one and a cyan one.

findings.title: Border-radius chaos — 11 different corner radii with no scale
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json borderRadii: 11px (×49), 32px (×19), 16px (×14), 10px (×7), 18px (×7), 30px (×5), 23px (×5), 999px (×2), 50% (×2), plus 0px 0px 10px and 0px 0px 10px 10px. Nav dropdown items use 11px, top-level nav buttons use 10px, cards use 16px/32px, pills use 999px, and stray 18px/23px/30px appear with no pattern. 11px and 10px and 18px are visually indistinguishable yet coexist.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A disciplined system exposes 2–3 radii (e.g. 8px control / 16px card / 999px pill). Eleven ad-hoc values — including bizarre 23px and 11px — is the fingerprint of merging multiple template fragments, each carrying its own hard-coded radius, with no shared token. Humans building a system don't type '23px'.
findings.recommendation: Collapse to a 3-step radius token set: --r-sm: 10px (controls/nav), --r-lg: 16px (cards), --r-pill: 999px (chips/badges). Remove 11px, 18px, 23px, 30px, 32px, 50% and the two box-shadow-shaped 'radius' values. Apply via CSS variables so it can't drift again.

findings.title: Section rhythm is a mechanical full-bleed color-band stack of mismatched blocks
findings.evidence: Vertical scan of desktop-full.png (6215px tall): white hero-intro → BLACK 'Choose the engagement model' band → white 'Beyond delivery' → CYAN 'Built for ambitious teams' block sitting inside a BLACK band → BLACK 'Why teams keep building with us' stat grid → light-gray (#e6e6e6/#ececec) 'Power your roadmap' grid → CYAN final CTA → dark #1c1c26 footer. Each band uses a different card fill: engagement cards are near-black on black; stat cards are #1a1a1a on #000; roadmap cards are #e6e6e6 on light gray.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Cohesive pages carry a consistent surface language and use spacing/typography — not just background flips — to separate sections. Alternating white/black/cyan slabs with three different card fill colors reads as separately-authored sections pasted end to end. It's the visual equivalent of changing fonts every paragraph.
findings.recommendation: Reduce to two surfaces max (e.g. light and one dark) and let whitespace + a consistent card style carry section separation. If you keep a dark band, keep its card fill identical to the other dark band (unify #1a1a1a vs near-#000). Don't introduce the #e6e6e6 gray card family at all — it belongs to a different template.

findings.title: Card families are inconsistent across sections — three different fills, and the stat grid has two different internal layouts
findings.evidence: backgrounds token counts: #000000 ×8, #1a1a1a ×4, #333333 ×4, #e6e6e6 ×6, #ececec ×5. In the 'Why teams keep building with us' 2×2 grid, the left cards ('500+ / PROJECTS DELIVERED', 'Real-time / OBSERVABILITY') place a huge left-aligned number in its own column with the heading offset to the right, while the right cards ('99.99% Enterprise reliability', '100% code ownership') stack number-on-top-of-heading in a single left column. Two layouts, one grid.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Cards in one grid must share one internal template. Mixing a two-column 'stat + description' layout with a stacked 'stat/heading/description' layout inside the same 2×2 block is a classic AI/templating artifact — the generator filled slots without enforcing a shared card component.
findings.recommendation: Define ONE stat-card component (icon or number, heading, body, link) and use it for all four. Unify the number typographic treatment ('500+', 'Real-time', '99.99%', '100%' currently vary in size/weight relationship to their label). Pick one card surface color for the whole page's card system.

findings.title: Card icons are payment-terminal / POS glyphs from the Verifone source library — wrong domain
findings.evidence: In the 'Choose the engagement model' cards, 'Fixed-scope projects' and 'Managed services' use card-reader/POS-terminal line icons; 'Dedicated teams' uses three linked POS terminals. In 'Power your roadmap', 'Reduce technical debt' repeats the three-POS-terminals icon and 'Accelerate time to market' uses a device+calendar glyph. These are hardware-payment-device icons, not software/cloud icons.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The iconography literally depicts credit-card terminals on a page selling React/Node/AWS engineering. It's direct residue of the lifted Verifone (payments company) template that nobody swapped out — the most concrete proof the design isn't original, and it silently undermines every credibility claim.
findings.recommendation: Replace the entire icon set with software/cloud-appropriate line icons (server, container, pipeline, shield, code brackets, cloud) from a single consistent icon family at one stroke weight and one grid size. The lone '</>' code-bracket icon in the 'Built for ambitious teams' block is the only on-brand icon and should set the style for the rest.

findings.title: Client-logo strip is under-populated and unevenly spaced — heavy right-side dead space
findings.evidence: Hero logo band (logos crop) shows only Bitdefender, 123 Form Builder, EaseUS, and Mondly. The Cisco and ViewSonic logos named in the metadata/claims are absent. The four present logos are pushed left/center with a large empty gap occupying roughly the right third of the full-bleed band, on a green gradient that also clashes with the cyan brand accent.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A four-logo 'trusted by' row stretched across a 1440px full-bleed with a third of it empty looks unbalanced and thin, and the green gradient background is yet another color not in the brand system. The mismatch between claimed logos and shown logos reads as hastily assembled placeholder content.
findings.recommendation: Either show a full, evenly-distributed logo set (justify-content with equal gaps, all logos optically sized to one height) or constrain the row to a centered max-width so there's no dead space. Drop the green gradient — put the logos on the same dark surface as the rest of the hero, or a neutral tint of the cyan brand.

findings.title: 'Beyond delivery' section pairs generic suit-and-tie stock photography with a full-bleed white gap
findings.evidence: The 'Beyond delivery - engineering as a long-term partnership' block uses an Unsplash-style photo of four people in business attire reviewing printed bar-chart paper in a glass-walled office. The 'Custom software' accordion section (top) and the engagement-image tile leave large white/empty right-hand regions (visible in d3/d4 crops), and the hero itself uses stock photography of a woman at a laptop.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Printed-chart-in-blazers stock imagery is the visual cliché of AI-generated/template corporate sites and is tonally wrong for a developer agency (engineers don't review printed bar charts in suits). Combined with the ragged whitespace balance, the section feels padded rather than composed.
findings.recommendation: Swap stock imagery for authentic product/engineering visuals (real dashboards, architecture diagrams, code, the actual team) or purpose-shot photography. Tighten the asymmetric layouts so image and text columns share a defined grid rather than leaving one side blank.

findings.title: Nav-control radius and padding drift — 10px vs 11px, dropdown items subtly misaligned
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json: top-level nav items ('Products','Solutions','Company') use radius 10px, padding 10px 13px, fontSize 15.36px; every dropdown item ('Software Development…','SaaS Development…', etc.) uses radius 11px, padding 11px 13px, fontSize 15.2px. So the parent and its children differ by 1px in radius, 1px in vertical padding, and 0.16px in font size.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Sub-pixel and 1px deltas between a menu and its own dropdown items (10px vs 11px, 15.36 vs 15.2) are exactly what you get from stitching two component sources rather than one token set. No human hand-picks 15.36px and 15.2px for the same menu.
findings.recommendation: Normalize all nav/menu controls to one radius (10px), one padding, and one font size (round the odd 15.36/15.2/15.2px values to a single 15px or 16px token). These fractional px sizes throughout (13.12px ×53, 14.4px, 15.2px) should be rounded to a real type scale.

result.overall_verdict: The copy is grammatically clean and, sentence by sentence, more competent than most agency sites — but it reads as AI-generated and non-enterprise for three structural reasons. First, there is no single owned positioning: the metadata layer alone carries THREE different taglines ("Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence" in the homepage title, "Engineering That Scales Your Business" in every og:title, "Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." in every og:image:alt), and the on-page hero adds a fourth promise ("the software and cloud platforms your business runs on"). A real brand ships one line everywhere; this reads like three model outputs that were never reconciled. Second, the copy leans on the exact cliché stack that signals LLM authorship in B2B — "with confidence" (used in the homepage title, a homepage card heading "Scale with confidence," and the identical CTA "Ready to build your next product with confidence?" repeated verbatim on the homepage, pricing, about, and software-development pages), plus "engineering partner, not a vendor," "long-term partnership," and "built to scale." Third, almost nothing is falsifiable or specific to Sentrize: the value props ("systems that keep performing as you grow," "an asset rather than becoming technical debt") are things any competitor could and does say. The information architecture actively undermines the enterprise claim: a 7-item top nav with mega-menus containing 7, 12, 8, 5, 6, 6, and 5 links (49 destinations) mislabeled as "Products" (services) and "Solutions" (also services), where the entire Pricing mega-menu's five items all point to the single /pricing URL. The strongest, most human copy on the site — the About "Our story" and the software-dev testimonial with concrete numbers ("40 minutes to deploy," "multiple times a day on Kubernetes") — proves the team CAN write specifically; the rest hasn't been held to that bar.

findings.title: Three conflicting taglines in metadata plus a fourth on-page — no owned positioning
findings.evidence: Homepage <title>: "Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence | Sentrize". og:title on ALL five pages: "Sentrize | Engineering That Scales Your Business". og:image:alt on ALL five pages: "Sentrize — Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." The on-page hero H1 says a fourth thing: "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on." Four distinct value promises, none repeated as the canonical line.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A company with real positioning uses ONE tagline consistently across title, OG, and hero — it is the single most-repeated string on a professional site. Carrying three different taglines in the machine-readable layer (which the founders likely never looked at) is the fingerprint of copy generated in separate passes and pasted without a style owner reconciling them. "Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." in particular is generic AI-brochure phrasing that means nothing and matches nothing else on the site.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE positioning and propagate it to title, og:title, og:image:alt, and hero. Of the four, "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on" is the strongest — it is concrete, has a point of view (mission-critical systems), and is the hardest for a competitor to copy. Kill "Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." entirely. Set every og:title to "Sentrize — the software and cloud platforms your business runs on" and make page <title> tags page-specific + brand ("Custom Software Development | Sentrize") which is already correct on the sub-pages; only the homepage title needs to change from the third variant to the chosen line.

findings.title: "With confidence" cliché repeated across the site, including a verbatim-duplicated CTA on four pages
findings.evidence: "...With Confidence" (homepage title); homepage Section 7 card heading "Scale with confidence"; and the identical closing CTA "Ready to build your next product with confidence?" appears verbatim on homepage (h2 class="black buffer0 h1_52"), pricing, about, and software-development. Its sub-line is near-duplicated too: homepage says "...we'll show you the fastest reliable path to production" while pricing/about/software-dev say "...we'll map the fastest reliable path to production."
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: "With confidence" is one of the top tells of LLM-written B2B copy — it adds emotional reassurance while committing to nothing measurable. Repeating the same CTA sentence on four pages (with a one-word map/show variation that itself betrays a generation seam) reads as a template filled by a model, not written by a team that reviews each page. Enterprise buyers register the repetition as thin.
findings.recommendation: Replace each closing CTA with a page-specific, outcome-anchored line. Software-dev page: "Ready to replace the system that breaks under load? Tell us your stack and we'll scope a rebuild." Pricing: "See a real estimate before you commit — book a scoping call." Homepage: "Tell us what you're shipping. We'll map the path to production and what it costs." Delete "with confidence" everywhere; rename the homepage card "Scale with confidence" to something falsifiable like "Auto-scaling that survives traffic peaks."

findings.title: Nav mislabeling: "Products" and "Solutions" both list services; "Pricing" mega-menu is five links to one URL
findings.evidence: Top nav has 7 mega-menus. "Products" (href=/services) lists Software Development, SaaS Development, Web Applications, Mobile Applications, API Development, AI & Automation, UI/UX Design — these are services, not products. "Solutions" (href=/solutions) lists DevOps, Cloud Infrastructure, AWS Consulting, Kubernetes, CI/CD, etc. — also services. The "Pricing" mega-menu's five entries (Development Plans, DevOps Plans, Managed Cloud, Dedicated Team, Enterprise) every one hrefs to "/pricing". "Company" is the visible label but the mega opens with "About Us".
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: An agency that sells services calling its services "Products" is a category error a real B2B marketer would never make — it signals the IA was scaffolded by a model reaching for generic SaaS nav labels. Five sub-menu items that all resolve to the same page is a classic AI-generated-site artifact: it manufactures the appearance of depth ("Enterprise", "Managed Cloud") behind pages that don't exist. Buyers who click and land on the same pricing page repeatedly lose trust fast.
findings.recommendation: Rename "Products" to "Services" (or "What we build") and "Solutions" to "Cloud & DevOps" or "How we help" so the two menus are clearly differentiated by domain, not by an invented product/solution split. Either build the five distinct pricing sub-pages or collapse the Pricing mega-menu to a single link — a menu of identical destinations is worse than no menu.

findings.title: Nav bloat: 49 destinations across 7 mega-menus, many pointing to pages that read as stubs
findings.evidence: Seven top-level mega-menus with 7, 12, 8, 5, 6, 6, and 5 items = 49 links. "Solutions" alone has 12 (DevOps, Cloud Infrastructure, AWS Consulting, Kubernetes, CI/CD, Managed Hosting, Infrastructure Monitoring, Security & Compliance, Cloud Migration, Docker Containerization, Infrastructure as Code, Managed DevOps). "Support" offers Help Center, Open a Ticket, Service Status, SLA Policy, Customer Portal, Contact Support; "Partners" appears both as its own top-level menu AND inside "Company".
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A ~120-person agency does not need a 49-link IA with a Customer Portal, Reseller Program, White Papers, and API Docs — this is the surface area of a large SaaS platform, not a services firm. Over-scaffolded navigation with duplicate entries (Partners in two places) is a hallmark of AI generation optimizing for apparent completeness. It signals to enterprise buyers that the site is a facade with more menu than substance.
findings.recommendation: Cut to 4-5 top-level items: Services, Cloud & DevOps, Work (portfolio/case studies), Pricing, Company. Fold Support and Partners into the footer until those programs genuinely exist. Every nav link should resolve to a page with unique, substantive content; remove any that would land on a stub.

findings.title: Value propositions are generic — nothing a competitor couldn't claim verbatim
findings.evidence: Section 2 h2: "An engineering partner built to scale your digital products." Section 4: "AN ENGINEERING PARTNER, NOT A VENDOR" / "Beyond delivery - engineering as a long-term partnership" / "We don't just ship code and walk away." Software-dev overview: software "stays an asset rather than becoming technical debt"; hero: "engineered to outlast its first release... instead of being rewritten in two years." About values are single generic nouns: "Engineering Rigor, Radical Ownership, Transparent Partnership, Security by Default, Pragmatic Innovation, Continuous Improvement."
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: "Partner not a vendor," "we don't just ship code and walk away," "built to scale," and "long-term partnership" are the four most common phrases in AI-written dev-agency copy — swappable onto any competitor's site with zero edits. The About "values" are the canonical LLM six-noun grid. None of it contains a proof point, a named client outcome, or a claim a rival couldn't equally assert, which is exactly why it reads as machine-filler rather than a real firm's voice.
findings.recommendation: Replace each abstract claim with a specific, verifiable one drawn from actual work. The site already proves this is possible: the software-dev testimonial ("a monolith that took 40 minutes to deploy... Eighteen months later we ship multiple times a day on Kubernetes with zero downtime") is concrete and credible. Rewrite the value props in that register — e.g., replace "Radical Ownership: Engineers own outcomes end to end" with a one-line story that shows it. If a sentence would be equally true on a competitor's site, cut or rewrite it.

findings.title: Section headings are vague and interchangeable rather than specific
findings.evidence: Software-dev page section headings: "What we deliver," "What is included," "How we work," "Technology stack," "Related services." Homepage: "Why teams keep building with us," "Built for ambitious technology teams," "Power your roadmap with the Sentrize engineering team," "Choose the engagement model that fits your team." About: "Principles we engineer by," "Our journey," "Small senior pods that own the whole system."
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: "What we deliver / What is included / How we work" is the default LLM section skeleton for a service page — generic labels that carry no information and could headline any service on any agency site. They make the page feel templated. (Note the About headings are notably better — "Small senior pods that own the whole system" is specific and human — showing the bar exists but isn't applied consistently.)
findings.recommendation: Make headings assert something. "What we deliver" → "Software modeled on your domain, not a template." "How we work" → "Reviewable increments, tested on every commit." "Why teams keep building with us" → lead with the proof: "500+ systems shipped, 99.99% uptime." A heading should be readable as a standalone claim, not a table-of-contents label.

findings.title: Em-dash / stylistic tells and unverifiable stat stack signal AI authorship
findings.evidence: Heavy em-dash and en-dash use in body copy: hero intro "pairing modern engineering practices with senior teams who stay for the long haul" follows an em-dash; software-dev "a generic template forced into shape" and "stays an asset rather than becoming technical debt" both hinge on em-dashes; About story "most software problems are really engineering-discipline problems." The About page stacks eight round-number stats (500+, 99.99%, 98%, 120+, 40+, 4.9/5, 12+, 24/7) and a suspiciously tidy timeline (2014→2026, ISO 27001 + SOC 2 Type II in 2019, AWS Advanced Tier 2022). Contact budget bands use en-dashes ($25k–$75k).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Frequent em-dashes mid-sentence to append a reassuring qualifier is one of the most recognizable LLM prose rhythms. Combined with a wall of perfectly round, unsourced metrics (99.99% uptime AND 98% retention AND 4.9/5 rating AND 500+ projects) and named awards from generic-sounding bodies ("Asia-Pacific Cloud Excellence Awards," "Global IT Services Council"), the effect is a site that asserts credibility numerically instead of demonstrating it — the pattern buyers now associate with generated content.
findings.recommendation: Cut the stat grid from eight to the three you can actually defend and would survive due diligence, and attribute them ("99.99% uptime across managed-cloud clients, 2024-2025"). Break up the em-dash cadence — convert half of them to full stops so the prose stops sounding uniform. Drop award lines you can't link to a verifiable source; a single Clutch profile link beats five unlinkable trophies.

findings.title: Reused/lifted template chrome contradicts the 'custom engineering' claim
findings.evidence: Homepage carries Verifone-template class names and copy scaffolding: hero uses "introPageBannerTitle," "modernCommerce," "introMobilDigital," "introMobileRetail" (retail/commerce IDs irrelevant to a dev agency), a "beyond-payments-image.png" asset in the 'partner not a vendor' section, and CSS files chrome.css / enterprise.css / en0000000000000812.css / theme-teal.css. Client logo marquee shows Cisco/Bitdefender/ViewSonic/EaseUS/Mondly/123FormBuilder linking to /success-stories; hero images are Unsplash stock (photo-1461749280684, photo-1498050108023).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A firm whose core pitch is "custom software engineered to outlast its first release" and "not a generic template forced into shape" (its own software-dev copy) is itself running on a visibly lifted Verifone commerce template with leftover retail IDs and a payments image. That contradiction is the single most damaging trust signal on the site — it tells a technical buyer the company doesn't practice what it sells. Stock Unsplash hero imagery and logo walls of companies with no visible case study compound the impression.
findings.recommendation: This is a build issue as much as copy: rename/replace the lifted class names and remove commerce-specific artifacts (beyond-payments-image.png, introMobileRetail). More urgently for messaging — either back the client logos with real, clickable case studies at /success-stories or remove them (unsubstantiated enterprise logos are a legal and credibility risk), and replace Unsplash stock with screenshots of actual delivered work. A custom-software firm's own site is its primary portfolio piece.

result.overall_verdict: This site reads as amateur and AI-generated on the trust dimension, and it carries real legal exposure. The "Trusted by" strip on the homepage displays six genuine, recognizable third-party brand logos (Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, 123 FormBuilder) — every one of them linking to /success-stories — yet the success-stories page never once mentions any of those companies; its case studies are anonymized fictional products (ClaimFlow, Loyalo, VitalLink). That is a textbook lifted "trusted-by" logo wall: the logos assert client relationships the site's own content cannot substantiate, and using competitors'/vendors' marks (Cisco, Bitdefender are security firms; ViewSonic/EaseUS/Mondly/123FormBuilder are consumer-software vendors — an implausible client roster for a no-footprint agency) to imply endorsement is trademark misuse and false advertising. Meanwhile the site's OWN invented proof is internally contradictory: the identical testimonial author appears under two different surnames on different pages, the company's CEO is also quoted as an external client, all 12 testimonials and 6 team members use no verifiable identity (stock Unsplash faces, no company names, no LinkedIn), every stat is an unsourced round number, awards and certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, AWS Advanced Tier, "Partner of the Year") are text-only with no certificate/verification link, and the contact page lists three prestige office addresses with a fake-reserved phone number and no company registration. A due-diligence buyer who spends 90 seconds cross-referencing pages will conclude the proof is fabricated.

findings.title: Real third-party brand logos (Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, 123 FormBuilder) presented as clients with zero corroboration — legal risk
findings.evidence: homepage.html lines 97-131: a marquee of , bitdefenderr-white.png (note the misspelled double-r filename), 123formbuilder-white.png, easeus-white.png, mondly-languages-white.png, viewsonic-white.png — each wrapped in . But page-success-stories.html contains NO occurrence of Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, or 123FormBuilder anywhere; its case studies are anonymized fictions (ClaimFlow, Loyalo, VitalLink, FleetSignal, LedgerCore, Triage AI). These specific brands are also the exact logo set shipped on Verifone.com-style marketing templates.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A firm claiming 500+ projects would name its marquee clients in a case study, not hide them behind fictional product names. The logos link to a page that never references them, which is the signature of a lifted template logo wall left un-swapped. Cisco and Bitdefender are themselves security/infrastructure vendors, and EaseUS/Mondly/ViewSonic/123FormBuilder are consumer-software companies — a wildly implausible and incoherent 'client' roster for an anonymous agency, which is the tell that these were grabbed for visual credibility rather than earned.
findings.recommendation: Remove all six third-party logos immediately — displaying Cisco/Bitdefender/etc. marks to imply a client relationship you cannot prove is trademark infringement and false advertising (deceptive-conduct exposure under Australian Consumer Law given the Sydney HQ claim). Replace with either (a) written, attributable client references you have signed permission to use, or (b) an honest 'Industries we serve' band with no logos. Never link a client logo to a page that doesn't feature that client.

findings.title: Same testimonial author appears under two different surnames; company CEO is quoted as an external client
findings.evidence: grep across files: 'Priya Venkataraman, CTO · E-commerce platform' in page-testimonials.html and page-success-stories.html, but 'Priya Venkatesan, CTO' in page-case-studies.html — same first name, two surnames, two industries. 'Marcus Hale, VP Engineering · Enterprise fintech' (testimonials/success-stories) vs 'Marcus Lindqvist, VP of Infrastructure' (case-studies). The surname 'Lindqvist' is then reused for a third person, 'Rachel Lindqvist, Founder · EdTech' (page-testimonials.html). Most damning: 'Sofia Marchetti' is the Chief Executive Officer on page-team.html ('Founded the company in 2014') AND is quoted as an external customer 'Sofia Marchetti, VP Engineering · Payments' in page-testimonials.html.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: These are LLM-generated placeholder names that were never reconciled across pages. No real testimonial set has the same person under two surnames, and no real company quotes its own founder as a third-party client. This is the single most decisive proof to a reviewer that every quote on the site is fabricated.
findings.recommendation: Delete every testimonial that cannot be tied to a real, consenting, named individual at a named company. If you have zero real testimonials, ship the page with none rather than fictional ones. At minimum, immediately fix the Venkataraman/Venkatesan and Hale/Lindqvist contradictions and remove the CEO (Marchetti) from the testimonial list — a diligence reviewer who catches this discards the entire site's credibility.

findings.title: Twelve testimonials with no company name, no photo, no verifiable person
findings.evidence: page-testimonials.html <cite> blocks: 'Daniel Okafor · Founder & CEO · B2B SaaS', 'Elena Sørensen · CISO · Healthtech', 'Tobias Reinhardt · Head of Product · Marketplace', 'Amara Diallo · Director of Operations · Logistics SaaS', etc. — 12 quotes, every attribution is a generic role plus a generic industry category. IMG count inside the testimonials <main> is 0 (no headshots), and there are no company names or LinkedIn links on any quote.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Real B2B testimonials name the company ('CTO, Xero') and usually carry a face and/or logo; anonymizing to 'CTO · E-commerce platform' is what you write when the client doesn't exist. The names are also a suspiciously even one-per-continent diversity spread (Okafor, Sørensen, Reinhardt, Diallo, Venkataraman, Nakamura, Boateng, Costa) — a hallmark of AI-generated persona lists.
findings.recommendation: For each quote add: full name, exact job title, real company name (with permission), and a real headshot or company logo. Cut the count to however many are genuinely real — three attributable, verifiable quotes outperform twelve anonymous ones. If none are real, remove the page and the /testimonials nav entry.

findings.title: Entire 'team' is stock Unsplash portraits with unverifiable identities
findings.evidence: page-team.html: six members — Sofia Marchetti (CEO), Rahul Desai (CTO), Klara Novak (Head of Cloud & DevOps), Benjamin Asante (Lead Software Architect), Yuki Tanaka (Head of Security), Olivia Brandt (Head of Design) — each rendered from images.unsplash.com stock photos (e.g. photo-1494790108377-be9c29b29330, one of the most-reused stock headshots on the web; photo-1500648767791; photo-1507003211169). No LinkedIn links, no last-name-searchable credentials, no bylines. Bio text is generic ('deep experience in Node.js, TypeScript, and distributed systems on AWS').
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A 120+ engineer firm founded in 2014 would have a real, linkable leadership team. Stock-photo faces with no LinkedIn is the clearest 'this company may not employ these people' signal, and photo-1494790108377 is instantly recognizable to anyone who has seen a template demo. Combined with the same face-set diversity pattern as the testimonials, it reads machine-assembled.
findings.recommendation: Use real photographs of real employees with links to their LinkedIn/GitHub profiles. If the company is too small/early to show six leaders, show the two or three real founders honestly. Reverse-image-searchable stock faces on a leadership page are disqualifying for enterprise buyers who vet vendors.

findings.title: All headline stats are unsourced round numbers repeated verbatim across every page
findings.evidence: Identical stat block ('500+ Projects delivered', '99.99% SLA-backed uptime', '98% Client retention', '120+ Engineers & specialists') appears on homepage.html (lines 707/746 etc.), page-success-stories.html, page-case-studies.html, page-about.html and page-team.html. Success-stories/about extend it with '40+ Countries served', '4.9/5 Average client rating', '12+ Years in operation', '24/7 Support coverage'. og:description hard-codes '120+ engineers, 500+ projects, and 99.99% SLA-backed uptime across 40+ countries.' None link to a methodology, status page, or third-party source; '99.99% uptime' has no public status page despite a /service-status nav link.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Every figure is a marketing-round number (500+, 120+, 40+, 99.99%, 98%, 4.9/5) with no denominator or source. '99.99% uptime' is a per-service SLA metric, not a company-wide brag, and stating it with no live status page is a red flag to any engineer. Repeating the exact same numbers on six pages with zero variance signals a single templated data blob, not measured reality.
findings.recommendation: Cut vanity stats you cannot substantiate. Keep only figures you can defend and source them (e.g. '99.9% uptime — see live status' linking to a real status page; 'client retention measured over trailing 24 months'). Replace '500+ projects' with 3-5 named, dated, verifiable engagements. Anchor '4.9/5' to a real Clutch/G2 profile or delete it.

findings.title: Awards and certifications are decorative text with no verification, evidence, or dates that survive scrutiny
findings.evidence: page-about.html 'Awards': 'Cloud Engineering Partner of the Year 2019 · Asia-Pacific Cloud Excellence Awards', 'Top B2B Software Developers 2021 · Clutch Global Recognition', plus Enterprise Technology Awards 2022, Global IT Services Council 2023, International Workplace Excellence Awards 2025 — all rendered as a '★' glyph with no logo, link, or citation. 'Certifications': ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 9001, AWS Advanced Tier, GDPR Compliant, HIPAA-Ready — each shown as a two-letter monogram in a coloured box ('I2', 'S2', 'AA', 'GC'), not an issuer's badge, with no certificate number or verification link. Timeline claims 'Achieved ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II' in 2019 and 'AWS Advanced Tier Partner' in 2022.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Award bodies like 'Asia-Pacific Cloud Excellence Awards' and 'Global IT Services Council' are not recognizable, and the entries have the generic, plausible-but-ungoogleable shape of AI-invented accolades. Real SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / AWS Advanced Tier status is verifiable (AWS Partner Finder, auditor letters) and firms link to it; substituting a hand-typed 'S2' monogram for the actual AWS/SOC badge is what you do when you can't produce the certificate. Claiming HIPAA/SOC 2/ISO without evidence is itself a compliance-misrepresentation risk.
findings.recommendation: Remove any certification you cannot prove. For real ones, embed the issuer's official badge and link to the verification record (AWS Partner Finder listing, ISO certificate number, SOC 2 report available under NDA). Drop unrecognizable award names or link each to the awarding body's published results page. Unverifiable trust badges are worse than none — they invite the exact 'this is fake' conclusion.

findings.title: Case-study metrics are impressive but wholly anonymous — no client is ever named
findings.evidence: page-case-studies.html leads with hero metrics ('3 wks→2 hrs release lead time', '900→12k TPS', '840ms→64ms p99 latency', '−44% monthly cloud spend') but every subject is a category, not a company: 'A Series C fintech offering embedded payments', 'A mid-market healthcare analytics company', 'A venture-backed streaming startup'. Quotes are attributed to the same unverifiable/contradictory names flagged above (Priya Venkatesan, Marcus Lindqvist, Sophie Aldridge, Reuben Castellanos, Hannah Brückner).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The writing is competent and the metrics are internally plausible, which is precisely the AI-generated 'realistic filler' pattern — specific enough to look real, anonymized enough to be unfalsifiable. Genuine case studies name the client (or state 'name withheld under NDA' with a real logo elsewhere); a whole page of nameless engagements plus fabricated quote-authors is not verifiable proof.
findings.recommendation: Convert at least one or two case studies into fully named references with a client contact willing to be cited, or clearly label the page 'representative engagements, details anonymized under NDA' and pair it with real named logos elsewhere. Do not attach fabricated named quotes to anonymized studies — the mismatch is what exposes the fabrication.

findings.title: Fictional-reserved phone number and geography mismatch on the contact page
findings.evidence: page-contact.html: 'Phone: +1 (628) 555-0137'. The 555-01XX block is the reserved directory range designated for fictional use (film/TV), so it is not a working business line. The number uses US country code +1 and area code 628 (San Francisco), yet the same page lists the primary office as 'Sydney, Australia — Level 12, 580 George Street, Sydney NSW 2000' plus 'London EC1M 3HE' and 'Singapore 049910'. Emails are generic role aliases (sales@sentrize.com, support@sentrize.com).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A US fictional-range number on an Australian-headquartered firm is a copy-paste placeholder that was never replaced — an unmistakable tell. 580 George Street and 9 Battery Road are well-known prestige/serviced-office towers, so listing them without a suite-level tenant identity reads as borrowed prestige addresses rather than a real footprint.
findings.recommendation: Replace the 555-01XX number with a real, answerable line in the country of your actual HQ. Ensure the phone's country matches the primary office. If the addresses are virtual/serviced offices, either substitute your real operating address or drop the office list rather than imply three global offices you don't staff.

findings.title: No company registration / legal entity anywhere, despite claiming a Sydney HQ and enterprise credibility
findings.evidence: grep across all pages: no ABN, ACN, 'Pty Ltd', company number, or VAT/registration identifier appears in any file. Footer (all pages) shows only '© 2026 Sentrize. All rights reserved.' with links to /privacy-policy, /terms-of-service, /sla-policy. The contact page asserts an Australian principal office ('Sydney NSW 2000') but no ABN — which Australian businesses are expected to display — and no registered legal entity name is given anywhere.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Legitimate enterprise vendors, especially Australian ones, publish their registered entity and ABN/ACN in the footer or contact page; its total absence alongside three claimed international offices signals either an unregistered operation or fabricated locations. Enterprise procurement and vendor onboarding will fail immediately without a verifiable legal entity.
findings.recommendation: Add the full registered legal entity name and jurisdiction plus ABN/ACN (and UK company number if the London office is real) to the footer and contact page. If no such entity exists, remove the international-office and enterprise claims until it does.

findings.title: No pricing transparency — figures exist only as ranges inside a lead-capture form
findings.evidence: page-pricing.html shows engagement tiers ('From $18k', 'Scale — From $14k/mo', 'Enterprise — Custom', 'Talk to sales') and a monthly/annual toggle, but the only concrete cost signals a prospect encounters are self-selected budget bands inside the contact form (page-contact.html: 'Under $25k / $25k–$75k / $75k–$200k / $200k+ / Not sure yet'). The homepage/nav 'Pricing' promises specifics that resolve mostly to 'Contact Us'/'Talk to sales'.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: This is a smaller trust issue than the fabricated proof — 'From $Xk' plus 'Enterprise: Custom' is a legitimate agency pattern. It reads slightly evasive only because the budget-selector in the contact form is doing the real qualification while the pricing page stays vague, but it is not a credibility red flag on its own.
findings.recommendation: Keep the 'From $X' anchors and add a one-line 'what's included / typical engagement size' under each tier so the pricing page carries more information than the contact form's budget dropdown. This is polish, not a fix for the trust problem — prioritize the logo, testimonial, and registration issues first.

result.overall_verdict: This reads as amateur-to-fraudulent for a firm that sells engineering. The page is a Next.js/turbopack shell (turbopack-3shn73yrjm21y.js, data-precedence="next") with an entire legacy Verifone.com template physically pasted on top (chrome.css, enterprise.css, en0000000000000812.css, theme-teal.css, sh__/sf__ classes). The lift is not merely stylistic: the "feature" icons are Verifone's payment-terminal "Blink" icon set embedded verbatim as base64 (data-iconname="TERMINAL_ENCRYPTION", "HARDWARE_HEALTH", "ESTATE_MANAGEMENT", "FIELD_SERVICES", "NETWORK_MANAGEMENT_SYSTEM"), a hero asset literally named beyond-payments-image.png (Verifone's "Beyond Payments" slogan), and dead jQuery ColorBox + a "#volume-booster-visusalizer" audio widget that have nothing to do with a software agency. On top of that sit real, checkable defects: three different taglines across title/OG/OG-alt, zero JSON-LD structured data, empty GTM shells, malformed cache-bust query strings, a pricing page with no canonical and an og:url pointing at the homepage, no image lazy-loading, ~15 of 31 images with no alt text, and a broken heading hierarchy. Any engineer opening DevTools sees a firm that sells clean architecture shipping the messiest possible front-end. This is the dimension most likely to lose a technical buyer, because the code is the product demo.

findings.title: Verifone payment-terminal icons shipped verbatim as base64 in a software-agency page
findings.evidence: The Section-3 pricing cards and Section-7 feature cards use blobs that decode to SVGs carrying class="blink-icon" and data-iconname attributes from Verifone's 'Blink' terminal UI kit. Enumerated across the 15 base64 images: TERMINAL_ENCRYPTION, HARDWARE_HEALTH, SOFTWARE_HEALTH, ESTATE_MANAGEMENT, FIELD_SERVICES, NETWORK_MANAGEMENT_SYSTEM, SINGLE_SOURCE_PROVIDER, DEEP_PERSONALIZATION, TWO_WAY_CONVERSATION, DESKTOP_PC, CALENDAR, and a source-baked typo OTHER_POLCICIES. Internal CSS ids inside the SVGs are .cls-2906. The SVGs even include blink-open/blink-shut animation groups (an eye that blinks) native to Verifone's brand system.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: These are icons for managing a fleet of physical card-payment terminals (terminal encryption, hardware/estate management, field services). Pasting them unmodified into a cloud-engineering agency page is nonsensical and is unambiguous proof the whole template was lifted, not built. The misspelling OTHER_POLCICIES shows nobody read what they copied. For a firm selling engineering rigor, this is the single most damaging artifact on the page.
findings.recommendation: Delete every base64 blink-icon and replace with a small, self-authored SVG set that maps to the actual services (custom software, SaaS, cloud/DevOps, security). Strip all data-iconname/blink-*/cls-2906 remnants. Do not embed 12+ large SVGs as base64 in HTML regardless — reference an inlined <symbol> sprite or optimized SVG files so the icon system is intentional and cacheable.

findings.title: Architectural incoherence: Next.js/turbopack app shell with an entire legacy Verifone stylesheet stack pasted on top
findings.evidence: The <head> loads a modern RSC/turbopack build (/_next/static/chunks/turbopack-3shn73yrjm21y.js, <link ... data-precedence="next">, a noModule="" legacy fallback chunk, and a ~188KB self.__next_f RSC hydration payload on the last line) AND simultaneously preloads/loads a hand-maintained legacy CSS stack: /assets/css/chrome.css, site.css, enterprise.css, en0000000000000812.css (an opaque Verifone build-hash filename), style.css, theme-teal.css. Body markup uses Verifone template classes: header.sh.sh--overlay, nav.sh__nav, sh__mega, footer.sf, sf__grid, plus content-CMS classes wrap_all homePage cookie-buffer, introPageBannerContainer, wrapperRingBanner, mouseGlowBox. Dead legacy plugins are still in the DOM: jQuery ColorBox (#cboxOverlay, #colorbox, #cboxWrapper ... #cboxSlideshow) and a #volume-booster-visusalizer audio visualizer block with an <audio class="audio-output"> and sound-wave segments.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A company that sells clean cloud architecture is shipping the least coherent front-end possible: a React 18 streaming shell wrapping a jQuery-era CMS theme it doesn't own, complete with a leftover audio-booster widget and a lightbox library that a Next.js build would never emit. en0000000000000812.css is literally another site's compiled asset name. Any prospective technical client who opens the Network/Elements tab immediately sees the product demo (the site itself) contradicts the sales pitch.
findings.recommendation: Pick one system. Rebuild the page as native Next.js components with a single owned stylesheet (or Tailwind/CSS modules) and delete chrome.css, enterprise.css, en0000000000000812.css, theme-teal.css, style.css, site.css and the sh__/sf__/wrap_all class taxonomy. Remove the ColorBox and volume-booster DOM entirely. The rendered HTML is your strongest sales asset for an engineering firm — treat it as production code, not a mashup.

findings.title: Three different brand taglines in the metadata of a single page
findings.evidence: homepage.html <head> carries three distinct positioning lines: <title>Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence | Sentrize</title>; og:title / twitter:title = "Sentrize | Engineering That Scales Your Business" (appears 4x); og:image:alt = "Sentrize — Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." (appears 2x). The <h1> in the body is yet a fourth line: "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on." The og:site_name is "Sentrize" but the twitter/og title format "Sentrize | ..." is inconsistent with the pipe order of the <title> ("... | Sentrize").
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A real brand ships one tagline. Three simultaneous, mutually-inconsistent slogans in one <head> is the classic fingerprint of AI-generated boilerplate (each field filled independently) or a template where placeholders were never reconciled. Search snippets and social shares will show a different promise than the page, undermining trust.
findings.recommendation: Choose one tagline and one title pattern (e.g. always "<Page> | Sentrize"). Make <title>, og:title, twitter:title identical, set og:image:alt to describe the actual OG image, and align the H1 to the same core promise. Do this across all pages.

findings.title: Canonical/HTTPS/www mismatch and pricing page missing canonical entirely
findings.evidence: Homepage declares <link rel="canonical" href="https://www.sentrize.com"> and og:url https://www.sentrize.com, but per the audit context the site is actually served at apex http://sentrize.com (no HTTPS, no www) — a three-way mismatch (scheme + host + trailing form). The pricing page (page-pricing.html) has NO canonical link at all, yet its og:url is content="https://www.sentrize.com" — i.e. the pricing page tells crawlers its canonical social URL is the homepage, not /pricing. No <meta name="robots"> on either page.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Serving a canonical that points to a URL the server doesn't actually redirect to (http apex vs https www) causes crawlers to see the live URL as non-canonical and can suppress indexing; an og:url that points every page at the homepage is a copy-paste tell. For a firm that lists 'Cloud Migration' and 'Security & Compliance' as services, shipping a site with no HTTPS is self-refuting.
findings.recommendation: Serve everything over HTTPS, 301-redirect apex+http to a single canonical host, and make each page's canonical + og:url self-referential to its own final HTTPS URL (e.g. https://www.sentrize.com/pricing). Add HSTS. Add a canonical to the pricing page. Verify the served host matches the declared canonical exactly.

findings.title: No structured data (JSON-LD) on either page
findings.evidence: grep for application/ld+json returns 0 matches in both homepage.html and page-pricing.html. There is no Organization, WebSite, BreadcrumbList, Service, or Offer schema anywhere, despite concrete claims (500+ projects, 120+ engineers, 99.99% uptime, 40+ countries) and a dedicated pricing page with engagement tiers.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: An engineering/SaaS vendor with a pricing page and named clients would normally ship Organization + Offer/Service schema; its total absence is a basic SEO miss that signals the metadata was generated superficially (OG/Twitter tags present, but no machine-readable entity data).
findings.recommendation: Add Organization JSON-LD (name, logo, sameAs to the LinkedIn/GitHub/X profiles already in the footer) on all pages, WebSite schema with SearchAction, and Service/Offer schema on the pricing page. Only include stat claims in schema if they are actually substantiated.

findings.title: Empty Google Tag Manager scaffolding left in place
findings.evidence: homepage.html lines 3-5 contain the GTM comment shell with nothing between: '<!-- Google Tag Manager (noscript) -->' then a blank line then '<!-- End Google Tag Manager (noscript) -->'. There is no corresponding GTM <script> in the head and no container id anywhere. The pricing page has no GTM comments at all (grep count 0), so the two pages are inconsistent about even the placeholder.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Empty analytics boilerplate is a hallmark of a template that was populated by find-and-replace and never finished. Either analytics were never wired up (so the business is flying blind) or the comments are vestigial from the source template. Inconsistency between pages confirms hand-assembly rather than a shared layout component.
findings.recommendation: Either install a real GTM/analytics container via a shared layout component so it appears identically on every page, or remove the dead noscript comments entirely. Do not ship half-wired third-party scaffolding.

findings.title: Malformed, self-referential cache-busting query strings on icons/favicons
findings.evidence: Icon links use query strings that repeat the filename plus a random token and duplicate the extension: rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico?favicon.2elyky-un3itm.ico", rel="icon" href="/icon.png?icon.0tey8b0o0tkkx.png", rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-icon.png?apple-icon.0oi4038m5n36y.png". The query value is not a version/hash param (e.g. ?v=abc123) — it is 'favicon.<token>.ico', an extension-suffixed filename used as a bare query string.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: This is not how any framework or CDN generates cache-busted assets; the query key/value is nonsensical (the whole thing is a value with no key) and duplicates the extension. It reads like an automated tool blindly appended hashed filenames into the query position — sloppy and inauthentic for an engineering vendor.
findings.recommendation: Use standard content-hash cache busting (e.g. /favicon.<hash>.ico as the path, or ?v=<hash> as a proper key=value) and let the framework generate it. Fix the icon URLs so they resolve to real files without the duplicated extension in the query.

findings.title: Broken heading hierarchy on both pages; H2s styled to imitate H1
findings.evidence: Homepage document order: H1 (introPageBannerTitle font_52) -> H2 -> H2 -> H3 -> H2 -> H2 -> H2 (class="black buffer0 h1_52", i.e. an H2 dressed as an H1) -> then footer H4 x4 (jumping H2 to H4, skipping H3). Pricing page order is worse: H1 -> H3 -> H3 -> H3 -> H2 -> H2 -> H2 -> H4 x4 — the first sub-heading after the H1 is an H3 (skips H2), and H2s appear only after the H3s.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Levels are chosen by visual size (font_44, h1_52) rather than document structure, and the pricing page opens H1 -> H3. This breaks the outline for screen readers and SEO and is a telltale sign headings were dropped into a copied template by appearance, not semantics.
findings.recommendation: Make headings follow a strict outline (single H1, then H2 for each major section, H3 for nested items, no skipped levels) and decouple size from level using CSS classes. Never use an H2 with an 'h1_52' class to fake an H1; size it with CSS instead.

findings.title: No image lazy-loading and missing alt text on ~15 of 31 images
findings.evidence: 31 tags total in homepage.html, 0 with loading="lazy". The client 'marquee' duplicates 12 logo s (Cisco, Bitdefender, 123 FormBuilder, EaseUS, Mondly, Viewsonic, each rendered twice for the infinite-loop effect) all eager-loaded. ~15 img tags have no alt attribute at all — these are the decorative base64 blink-icons, which are neither given alt="" nor aria-hidden, so screen readers will announce raw/empty images. One logo asset is misspelled bitdefenderr-white.png (double r).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Shipping 31 eager images including a 12-image duplicated marquee with no lazy-loading, plus unlabeled decorative icons, is exactly the accessibility/perf debt an engineering firm claims to eliminate for clients. The misspelled asset filename reinforces the copy-paste-without-review pattern.
findings.recommendation: Add loading="lazy" to all below-the-fold images (all marquee logos and the platform mockup), add width/height to prevent CLS (some already have it), give decorative icons alt="" plus aria-hidden="true", and give the client logos meaningful alt like 'Cisco logo'. Use next/image so this is handled automatically, and fix the bitdefenderr-white.png filename.

findings.title: Unsupported quantitative claims stated as fact with no substantiation, next to lifted client logos
findings.evidence: meta description and body assert '120+ engineers, 500+ projects, and 99.99% SLA-backed uptime across 40+ countries' (Section 6: font_52 '500+' PROJECTS DELIVERED, '99.99%' Enterprise reliability, 'SLA-backed uptime', 'Real-time OBSERVABILITY', '100% code ownership'). The client marquee shows Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, 123 FormBuilder as logos linking to /success-stories — the same logo set that appears on the source Verifone-style template, with no case study text on the homepage to back any relationship.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Round, unverifiable numbers (500+/120+/99.99%/40+) combined with major brand logos and zero substantiating detail is the signature of AI-generated marketing filler. Because the rest of the page is provably a lifted template, a technical evaluator will assume these stats and logos are fabricated too, which is worse than omitting them.
findings.recommendation: Either back each claim with a verifiable artifact (public status page for 99.99%, named case studies for the logos with client permission, a team page listing the engineers) or remove/soften the numbers. Do not display client logos you cannot prove a relationship with — for an engineering buyer, unbacked logos read as fraud.

result.overall_verdict: This page reads as amateur and template-lifted the moment you interact with it non-visually or on a phone.
