Accessibility is broken in ways an enterprise agency would never ship: the skip link points to #main but no such element (and no <main> landmark) exists, so it silently does nothing; the mobile hamburger has aria-expanded but no aria-controls and there is literally no mobile menu markup for it to open (the sh__menu is the desktop Verifone mega-menu); the entire page has exactly ONE real subheading (a single <h3>) while every stat and feature-card title is a non-semantic <div>, so a screen-reader heading outline is essentially h1 → 5×h2 and nothing else; and the site's most-used text color #5b5b6b (49 uses) sits on near-black cards at ~2.5:1 contrast, failing WCAG AA and even the 3:1 large-text floor. On mobile the 6215px desktop design becomes a ~9,800px endless scroll built from ~16 near-identical dark rounded cards each ending in an identical "Learn more" link (48 total, 24 unique, all the same words), interrupted by jarring full-bleed black↔white↔cyan band flips. Combined with leftover Verifone template cruft (beyond-payments-image.png, colorbox dialog, a "volume-booster" audio widget) and three conflicting taglines in the metadata, the experience confirms rather than dispels the "AI-generated, non-professional" impression.

findings.title: Skip link targets #main but no #main / <main> element exists — skip-to-content is broken
findings.evidence:
Line 1 emits `Skip to content`, and design-tokens lists it as a real focusable button (bg #0a84ff). But a full scan of homepage.html returns 0 occurrences of `id="main"` and 0 `
` elements. The main content wrapper is `
` / `
` with no id. The hero uses `id="banner"`, not `main`.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A skip link is the single most visible signal that someone thought about keyboard/screen-reader users. Shipping one whose anchor goes nowhere is worse than not having it — it's the classic signature of scaffolding copied without wiring it up. Enterprise sites test this; template-lifters don't.
findings.recommendation: Add `id="main"` (and ideally convert the wrapper to a semantic `<main id="main" tabindex="-1">`) on the primary content container at line 6/39 so the skip link and the landmark both resolve. Verify by tabbing once from page load and pressing Enter — focus must jump past the 7-item nav.

findings.title: Mobile hamburger button opens nothing — aria-expanded present, aria-controls absent, no mobile menu markup
findings.evidence: Line 15: `<button class="sh__toggle" aria-label="Menu" aria-expanded="false">☰</button>`. There are 0 `aria-controls` attributes in the entire document, and the only menu is `<ul class="sh__menu">` — the desktop Verifone-style mega-menu (`sh__mega sh__mega--wide`) containing ~50 links across 7 columns. There is no separate mobile drawer/nav in the DOM. The mobile screenshot shows the ☰ icon top-right but a mobile user has no primary navigation path.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: On a phone the entire multi-level site navigation (Products, Solutions, Company, Pricing, Resources, Support, Partners) is unreachable — you can only scroll the landing page. A working enterprise site's #1 requirement is that mobile users can navigate. The static aria-expanded="false" that never toggles is a hallmark of markup generated/copied without the accompanying JS.
findings.recommendation: Ship an actual mobile nav: on toggle, reveal a full-screen or drawer menu built from the same link data, set `aria-controls="<menu-id>"` on the button, toggle `aria-expanded` true/false in JS, move focus into the menu, and trap/close on Escape. Until then the site is unusable on mobile beyond the homepage.

findings.title: Primary body text color #5b5b6b fails WCAG contrast on the dark cards it's used on (2.5:1)
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json lists #5b5b6b as the most-used text color (49 occurrences) and the card/section backgrounds include #1a1a1a, #1c1c26, #333333 and #000000. Computed contrast: #5b5b6b on #1c1c26 = 2.54:1, on #1a1a1a = 2.61:1, on #000000 = 3.15:1 — all below the 4.5:1 AA minimum for body text and below the 3:1 large-text floor for the smaller sizes (13.12px is the 2nd-most-common size). Separately #9a9aad on white = 2.76:1 (fails), and brand cyan #0891b2 on white 'Learn more' links = 3.68:1 (fails AA).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Mid-grey body copy on near-black is the default 'looks moody' choice of an AI/template that never ran a contrast checker. On the mobile crops the paragraph text under 'Full visibility', '100% code ownership', and the footer link columns is visibly washed out. Enterprise design systems pin body text to a token that passes AA on every surface.
findings.recommendation: Raise dark-surface body text to at least #b8b8c4 (≈7:1 on #1a1a1a) and light-surface body text off #9a9aad to ~#5b5b6b (6.66:1 on white). Recolor cyan links to #0e7490 or darker on white. Define one AA-verified 'text-secondary' token per surface instead of reusing #5b5b6b on both.

findings.title: No heading hierarchy — one lone <h3>, and every stat/card title is a non-semantic <div>
findings.evidence: Grep of all heading tags returns: one <h1> (line 53), five <h2> (154, 338, 696, 858, 1077), and exactly one <h3> (line 459, 'Beyond delivery'). The stat cards render '500+' and 'Proven delivery' as `<div class="font_52 ...">` and `<div class="flexBoxTitle">` (lines 706–718); the six 'Power your roadmap' card titles ('Scale with confidence', etc.) are `<div class="flexBoxTitle font_24">` (line 871); the six service-accordion labels ('Web & mobile apps', etc.) are `<div class="fontRegular_24">`. Footer column headers use <h4> but there is no <h3> level bridging them.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A screen-reader user pulling up the heading list gets h1, five identical-styling h2s, and a single orphan h3 — no way to grasp the page's ~7 distinct card sections. Styling text large with a font_52/font_24 class instead of using headings is the exact shortcut an AI/template generator takes. It also tanks SEO structure for a company selling 'engineering.'
findings.recommendation: Promote card titles to real headings: stat/value labels and the six 'Power your roadmap' and six engagement-model titles should be <h3> (or <h4> nested under their section <h2>). Keep the visual size via classes, but the tag must convey structure.

findings.title: 15 of 31 images have no alt attribute at all (not even alt="")
findings.evidence: Programmatic count: 31 total, 15 with NO alt attribute and 0 with empty alt. The missing-alt set is the base64 data-URI card icons (lines 350–670, 869–1033, e.g. ``) plus the leftover hero-adjacent `` (line 453). The logo (line 14/1107) and marquee client logos (98–131) and 'Platform designed to scale' (323) do have alt.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Screen readers announce a src-less-alt image by reading its filename/URL — here a 3KB base64 blob or 'beyond-payments-image.png'. Decorative icons should be alt="" (or aria-hidden); a content image needs a real description. Having exactly half the images un-alted is a generator artifact, not a human review.
findings.recommendation: Add `alt=""` to the decorative card icons (they duplicate adjacent text) and give beyond-payments-image.png a descriptive alt or mark it decorative. Note the filename itself ('beyond-payments') is Verifone-template residue and should be renamed.

findings.title: The 6215px desktop page becomes a ~9,800px endless mobile scroll of near-identical dark cards
findings.evidence: design-tokens pageHeight is 6215px desktop; the mobile screenshot is 390×9816px — the page grows ~58% taller and requires roughly 25 phone-viewport scrolls. The middle third (bands m04–m07) is a monotonous vertical stack: three stat cards, then six full-width 'Power your roadmap' cards, then more — each an identical dark or grey rounded rectangle (border-radius 11px/32px) with an icon, a title, 2–3 lines, and a 'Learn more'. 'Learn more' appears 48 times in the markup.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Everything is stacked at one rhythm with no density variation, no 2-up grids collapsed intelligently, no anchor navigation — the reader loses orientation. This 'one long ribbon of the same card' is the tell-tale output of generated section templates dumped in sequence. Real product sites vary layout and cut content for mobile.
findings.recommendation: For mobile, cut or collapse: turn the six-card 'Power your roadmap' and six-card engagement grids into a swipeable carousel or an accordion, drop redundant sections, and shorten the page. Vary card treatments so sections are distinguishable while scrolling.

findings.title: 24 identical 'Learn more' links give screen-reader users a useless, ambiguous link list
findings.evidence: 48 'Learn more' strings in markup = 24 rendered links (each is `...Learn more`, lines 187, 213, 239, 725, etc.). All say 'Learn more' with no distinguishing accessible name, and most point to the same `/services` URL. The SVG arrow is correctly aria-hidden, so the entire accessible name is just 'Learn more'.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: When a screen-reader user opens the links-list dialog they see 'Learn more' 24 times with no way to tell them apart — a WCAG 2.4.4 (Link Purpose) failure. Duplicating one generic CTA across every card, mostly to the same page, is generated-content behavior.
findings.recommendation: Give each link a unique accessible name, e.g. append visually-hidden context: `Learn more about custom software`, or use aria-label. Point them to their actual distinct destinations rather than all to /services.

findings.title: Jarring full-bleed band-color flips (black → green → white → cyan) with no transition on small screens
findings.evidence: Mobile crops show: hero ends in a green gradient behind Bitdefender/123FormBuilder logos (band m00), abruptly to a white section 'An engineering partner...' , then a black engagement-model section (m01–m02), a white photo section, then a solid cyan #22d3ee 'Built for ambitious technology teams' block (m03), back to black stats (m04), to white 'Power your roadmap' (m05), then a full cyan #22d3ee CTA block (m07), then dark footer. design-tokens confirms backgrounds #000000, #ffffff, #22d3ee, #0891b2 all in play.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The hard black/white/cyan alternation with no shared spacing or gradient bridging reads like separate templates pasted together — especially the two unrelated saturated-cyan blocks. On a narrow screen each flip fills the whole viewport, so the eye is strobed. Enterprise sites maintain a controlled 2–3 surface palette with intentional rhythm.
findings.recommendation: Reduce to a deliberate light/dark alternation, reserve the saturated cyan for one CTA moment only (not two full blocks), and add consistent vertical padding tokens between bands so transitions feel authored rather than concatenated.

findings.title: Three conflicting taglines in the page metadata
findings.evidence: Line 1 metadata contains: <title> 'Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence | Sentrize', og:title/twitter:title 'Sentrize | Engineering That Scales Your Business', and og:image:alt 'Sentrize — Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact.' Three different positioning statements ship simultaneously; the on-page hero H1 is a fourth phrasing ('We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on').
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A company that can't state one consistent tagline across its own <head> looks like copy was generated in multiple passes and never reconciled. Search/social previews will show a different slogan than the tab title. This is a direct 'AI-generated' tell.
findings.recommendation: Pick one tagline and use it verbatim in <title>, og:title, twitter:title, and og:image:alt. 'Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact.' in particular is generic filler that undercuts the concrete engineering positioning of the H1 — drop it.

findings.title: Leftover Verifone / unrelated template cruft left in the shipped DOM
findings.evidence: CSS files are the lifted set (chrome.css, enterprise.css, en0000000000000812.css, theme-teal.css) with header class sh__nav. The DOM still contains a jQuery Colorbox lightbox scaffold `<div id="colorbox" role="dialog" tabindex="-1">` with cboxPrevious/cboxNext/cboxSlideshow buttons (line 1132), a 'volume-booster-visusalizer' audio widget with sound-wave segments (lines 1132–1146, note misspelled class 'visusalizer'), an empty Google Tag Manager noscript comment (lines 3–5), and image filename 'beyond-payments-image.png' — payments being Verifone's domain, not Sentrize's.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A 'volume booster visualizer' and a payments-themed image on a software-agency homepage are unmistakable evidence of a copied template that was never cleaned. The misspelled class ('visusalizer') and empty GTM stub reinforce that no one audited the output. These are exactly the artifacts that make a site read as non-original / AI-assembled.
findings.recommendation: Strip the Colorbox scaffold, the audio 'volume-booster' widget, and empty GTM comments; rename Verifone-derived assets (beyond-payments-image.png) and CSS files to project-specific names; remove any theme classes (theme-teal, en0000000000000812) not actually used.

findings.title: No visible focus styling defined in the document; hero H1 uses ultra-thin 300 weight despite fontBold class
findings.evidence: The only 'focus'/'outline' references in homepage.html are inside the skip-link inline CSS (line 1) and the minified blob (line 1147); no component (nav links, the 24 'Learn more' links, CTA buttons, accordion toggles) has an authored :focus-visible style in the inline markup, so keyboard focus likely relies on unstyled defaults or is suppressed by the template reset. Separately, design-tokens shows the H1 (class 'fontBold') actually renders at weight 300 (57.2px line-height, 52px), and every H2 is weight 300 too — 'Bold' class producing hairline text.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Keyboard users need a clearly visible focus ring on all 24+ card links and nav items; template resets frequently kill the default outline, and nothing here restores it. And a class literally named fontBold rendering as thin 300 weight is a mismatched-token artifact typical of a lifted stylesheet where the font files (Verifone-Sans-Bold) aren't mapped to the class.
findings.recommendation: Add a global `:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #22d3ee; outline-offset: 2px; }` and verify every interactive element shows it. Fix the weight mapping so 'fontBold' hero text loads Verifone-Sans-Bold (or intentionally rename the class to match the 300 weight actually used).

findings.title:
Heading line-breaks use hardcoded
causing awkward orphans on mobile ('model' / 'team' alone on a line)
findings.evidence:
Headings hardcode desktop line breaks: line 339-341 'Choose the engagement model
that fits your team', line 859-861 'Power your roadmap with the
Sentrize engineering team', and the H1 lines 54-58 use three
. On the 390px mobile crop (m01) this renders 'Choose the engagement' / 'model' (orphan word alone) / 'that fits your team', and (m05) 'Power your roadmap with' / 'the Sentrize engineering' / 'team' (orphan).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai:
Hardcoded
line breaks are tuned for one desktop width and fall apart responsively, stranding single words ('model', 'team') on their own line. Hand-crafted enterprise sites use max-width + natural wrapping or text-wrap:balance; the

findings.recommendation:
Remove the
tags from headings and control line length with max-width plus `text-wrap: balance`, so wrapping adapts per viewport and no single-word orphans appear on mobile.

result.dimension: Brand credibility & "is this a real enterprise?" trust signals
result.overall_verdict: This page reads as amateur/AI-generated within about two seconds, and the reasons are concrete, not vibes. The single most damaging tell is forensic: the entire front-end is a lifted Verifone.com template. The brand font is literally "Verifone-Sans-Regular" (used 550 times per design-tokens.json), the stylesheets are named chrome.css / enterprise.css / en0000000000000812.css / theme-teal.css, and the header markup still uses Verifone's sh__nav class system. A real 120-engineer firm does not ship a competitor-payments-company's design system with the serial-numbered CSS filenames intact. Layered on top of that theft are the classic generative-content fingerprints: three mutually inconsistent taglines in the metadata, four suspiciously round trophy stats ("500+", "120+", "99.99%", "40+") with zero proof, a marquee of borrowed enterprise logos (Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, 123 FormBuilder) that link to a "/success-stories" page but are backed by no testimonial or case study anywhere on the homepage, a generic Unsplash stock hero, two competing accent color systems (teal vs blue), universally thin 300-weight headings, and a border-radius set that uses eleven different values. Individually each is a yellow flag; stacked together they scream "template + AI copy + fabricated proof." The good news: almost every tell is cheap to fix because the problem is presentation and honesty, not the underlying service offering.

findings.title: The entire site is a lifted Verifone.com template — font, CSS filenames, and class names all still say "Verifone"
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json fontFamilies: "Verifone-Sans-Regular, sans-serif" (550 uses), plus Verifone-Sans-Medium/SemiBold/Bold. homepage.html head preloads /assets/css/chrome.css, /assets/css/enterprise.css, /assets/css/en0000000000000812.css, and /assets/css/theme-teal.css; header uses class sh__nav (Verifone's shell-header convention). The og:image:alt is even "Sentrize — Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." over a stolen chrome.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: An enterprise buyer's technical evaluator opens DevTools and sees a payments giant's design system with its serialized CSS filename (en0000000000000812.css) shipped verbatim. It signals the company cannot produce its own front-end — fatal for a firm selling "custom software" and "UI/UX Design" — and it invites a trademark/brand-theft concern that instantly kills trust.
findings.recommendation: Rebuild the front-end on an original design system. Rename/rewrite CSS to semantic files (base.css, components.css), delete every Verifone-* font reference and self-host a licensed brand typeface (e.g. Inter, Söhne, or a commissioned face), and purge all sh__ / chrome / enterprise / en0000... artifacts. This is non-negotiable and must be fixed before anything else.

findings.title: Three different taglines in the metadata — the brand does not know what it is
findings.evidence: Verbatim from homepage.html head: <title>"Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence | Sentrize"; og:title/twitter:title "Sentrize | Engineering That Scales Your Business"; og:image:alt "Sentrize — Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." The H1 then adds a fourth positioning line: "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on."
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Inconsistent taglines across title/OG/social are a hallmark of copy generated in separate prompts and pasted without an editor. A buyer who sees the browser tab say one thing and the LinkedIn share preview say another concludes there is no marketing owner and no brand discipline — i.e. not a real 120-person company.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE positioning line and use it everywhere. "Intelligent Software. Limitless Impact." is the weakest (empty AI cadence); "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on" is the strongest and concrete. Standardize title, og:title, twitter:title, and hero to that single line.

findings.title: Four round-number trophy stats with zero proof, and they only appear in metadata + a stat block — never substantiated
findings.evidence: meta description: "120+ engineers, 500+ projects, and 99.99% SLA-backed uptime across 40+ countries." On-page stat cards (homepage.html ~line 707-828): "500+ / PROJECTS DELIVERED / Production systems shipped across 40+ countries", "99.99% / Enterprise reliability", plus "Real-time / OBSERVABILITY", "100% / code ownership". No dates, client names, or audited source anywhere.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Every number is a suspiciously clean round figure (500+, 120+, 40+, 99.99%, 100%) — the exact distribution an LLM emits when asked to "make it sound impressive." A 500-project / 40-country firm would be widely known; the absence of a single named engagement to back it reads as fabricated, which is worse than claiming nothing.
findings.recommendation: Replace unprovable round numbers with specific, verifiable facts: a real project count with a "since 2019" date, 2-3 named clients with a one-line outcome and metric, and a link to a status page for the 99.99% claim. If the numbers can't be substantiated, delete them — an honest smaller number beats an unbelievable big one.

findings.title: Borrowed enterprise logo strip with no case studies behind it — implies clients that aren't clients
findings.evidence: homepage.html lines 98-131: a duplicated marquee of Cisco, Bitdefender, 123 FormBuilder, EaseUS, Mondly, Viewsonic logos, each wrapped in <a href="/success-stories">. No testimonial, quote, engagement description, or named case study exists on the homepage — the grep for testimonial/case-study content returns only class names, not real content.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A row of famous logos with nothing behind them is the oldest fake-credibility trick, and buyers know it. Linking each logo to a generic /success-stories page with no per-client story reads as "we found these logos," not "these are our clients." For Cisco/Bitdefender-tier names this is also legally risky if untrue.
findings.recommendation: Show only logos you can prove, and pair each with a real artifact: a one-paragraph case study, a named quote with person + title + photo, or a measurable outcome. One genuine, detailed case study outperforms six borrowed logos. If these are not real clients, remove them entirely.

findings.title: Two competing accent-color systems — teal and blue fighting on the same page
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json shows teal accents #0891b2 (7 text + 3 bg uses) and #22d3ee (3 bg), sourced from theme-teal.css, alongside a separate blue family: #0a84ff (Skip-to-content button bg), #004aef, and #3474ff. The hero CTA and skip link are blue while section accents and the big cyan "Built for ambitious technology teams" panel are teal.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A coherent brand has one accent color. Teal (#0891b2/#22d3ee) from the borrowed theme-teal.css collides with leftover Verifone-style blues (#0a84ff/#004aef) — the visual signature of a template whose theme was half-recolored. It tells a designer's eye that nobody owns the palette.
findings.recommendation: Choose one accent and define it as a token (e.g. commit to teal #0891b2 with #22d3ee as the light variant, and eliminate #0a84ff/#004aef/#3474ff entirely, including the Skip-to-content button). Every button, link, and highlight should resolve to that single accent + one neutral scale.

findings.title: Everything is hairline-thin weight 300 — no typographic hierarchy or authority
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json headings: H1 52px weight 300, every H2 44px weight 300, H3 32px weight 300, final CTA H2 30px weight 300. Body text is dominated by low-contrast grays #5b5b6b (49 uses) and #9a9aad (31 uses) on white. (Note: the H1 markup class is fontBold but it renders at weight 300 per the computed tokens — a further style/markup mismatch.)
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Uniform 300-weight at every level means the H1, H2, and H3 differ only by pixel size, so there is no real hierarchy — a default AI/template aesthetic that mistakes "thin = premium." Combined with washed-out gray body copy (#9a9aad on white fails contrast), it looks fragile and unedited rather than confident and enterprise.
findings.recommendation: Establish weight contrast: headings at 600-700, body at 400, and darken body text to roughly #33333a or #1c1c26 for legibility. Reduce the H1 to a deliberate scale (e.g. 48-56px/700) and let weight — not just size — carry the hierarchy.

findings.title: Generic Unsplash stock hero — the literal "AI startup" cliché image
findings.evidence: homepage.html head: <link rel="preconnect" href="https://images.unsplash.com">; desktop-hero.png shows a generic stock photo of a woman at a laptop in front of dual monitors, framed in a borderRadius32 "video" card with a fake play button (introPagePlay markup, no actual video source).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Unsplash "person at laptop" is the single most overused stock image in AI-generated landing pages; buyers have seen it on a thousand template sites. The decorative play button that opens nothing compounds the impression of a mockup dressed up as a real product.
findings.recommendation: Replace with owned imagery: a real photo of the team/office, a screenshot of actual delivered software, or a purpose-built abstract brand graphic. If there is no video, remove the play button; if there is, wire it up. Never ship a stock human photo as the hero for an engineering brand.

findings.title: Border-radius and spacing chaos — eleven different corner radii across the page
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json borderRadii: 11px (49x), 32px (19x), 16px (14x), 10px (7x), 18px (7x), 30px (5x), 23px (5x), 999px (2x), 50% (2x), plus shadow-shaped values. Buttons alone mix radius 10px (nav), 11px (menu items), and a 0px 0px 10px skip-link. Font sizes are similarly untuned: 13.12px (53x), 15.36px, 15.2px, 14.4px — non-integer values that come from unscaled template rem math.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: A designed system uses a small radius scale (typically 2-3 values) and a clean type scale. Eleven radii and fractional font sizes like 13.12px / 15.36px / 15.2px are the fingerprint of a template being stretched and overridden piecemeal — the details a professional would never let ship.
findings.recommendation: Define a radius token scale of at most three values (e.g. 8px controls, 16px cards, 999px pills) and apply globally. Rebuild the type scale on whole-pixel steps (e.g. 14/16/20/24/32/44) so no computed size lands on 13.12px or 15.2px.

findings.title: Cliché, hollow AI-cadence copy throughout
findings.evidence: Verbatim: hero subcopy "pairing modern engineering practices with senior teams who stay for the long haul"; section "Beyond delivery - engineering as a long-term partnership"; "Why teams keep building with us"; "Built for ambitious technology teams"; "Ready to build your next product with confidence?"; service blurbs like "Kubernetes — Orchestration done right", "CI/CD Automation — Ship safely, many times a day", "Managed Hosting — We run it, you build it."
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: These are frictionless, interchangeable phrases with no specifics — the exact register of LLM marketing filler. "Orchestration done right" and "engineering as a long-term partnership" could belong to any of ten thousand agencies; they convey zero differentiated information, which readers now pattern-match to AI-written.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite with specifics only a real practitioner would know: name the stack decisions, the SLAs, the on-call model, a concrete client outcome ("cut deploy time from 40 min to 4"). Replace slogans with claims that carry a number, a name, or a mechanism.

result.dimension: Content credibility & trust conventions (positioning, proof, palette, typography, team) vs. credible dev/cloud consultancies
result.overall_verdict: Sentrize reads amateur/AI-generated because it violates nearly every convention that makes real consultancies (Thoughtworks, Netguru, STX Next) read as trustworthy. The most damaging tells are structural, not cosmetic: (1) the entire front-end is a lifted Verifone.com payments template — CSS files chrome.css / enterprise.css / en0000000000000812.css / theme-teal.css, header classes sh__nav / sh__mega, a Verifone-Sans brand font, and a leftover /assets/images/beyond-payments-image.png asset that belongs to a payments company, not a dev shop; (2) three different taglines ship simultaneously (title "Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence", H1 "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on", og:title "Engineering That Scales Your Business") where credible firms commit to exactly one; (3) unverifiable round-number brag stats (500+ projects, 120+ engineers, 99.99% uptime, 40+ countries) presented with zero named-client evidence; (4) real, recognizable client logos (Cisco, Bitdefender, ViewSonic, EaseUS, Mondly, 123FormBuilder) paired with case studies that name NO clients ("a Healthcare Data Provider", "a B2B Collaboration Product"); and (5) a "Our Team" page of named executives whose headshots are generic Unsplash stock portraits — the same photo IDs reused elsewhere on the site. Credible firms do the opposite of all five: one positioning line, named case studies with real metrics, real client logos that match named stories, real team photos, and a bespoke design system. Fixing the palette or typography alone will not help until these credibility contradictions are removed.

findings.title: Lifted Verifone.com payments template — filenames, font, and a leftover payments asset betray the origin
findings.evidence: homepage.html loads /assets/css/chrome.css, /assets/css/enterprise.css, /assets/css/en0000000000000812.css and /assets/css/theme-teal.css; header markup uses class="sh__nav", "sh__mega sh__mega--wide", "sh__brand", "sh__cta". design-tokens.json shows the brand font is Verifone-Sans-Regular (550 occurrences) / Verifone-Sans-Medium / -SemiBold / -Bold. A stock image /assets/images/beyond-payments-image.png (a Verifone 'Beyond Payments' marketing asset) is still referenced on the homepage. Credible firms ship a bespoke design system: Thoughtworks, Netguru and STX Next all use their own component/class naming and self-hosted brand fonts, never a competitor's payment-terminal template.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: The single strongest 'this was slapped together' signal. An enterprise-grade engineering firm that literally builds software would never ship a front-end whose class names (sh__), obfuscated stylesheet (en0000000000000812.css), brand font (Verifone-Sans) and orphaned image asset (beyond-payments) all point to a payments company it has no relationship with. It signals the site was cloned, not built, which is fatal for a firm selling engineering competence.
findings.recommendation: Strip every Verifone artifact before anything else. Rename CSS to semantic files (base.css, layout.css, components.css), delete en0000000000000812.css and beyond-payments-image.png, rename all sh__* classes to a Sentrize prefix (e.g. sz-nav, sz-mega), and replace Verifone-Sans with a self-hosted neutral grotesque you have a license to (e.g. Inter, or a licensed face like Söhne/Aeonik). Grep the repo for 'verifone', 'sh__', 'beyond-payments', 'chrome.css', 'enterprise.css' and confirm zero hits before launch.

findings.title: Three different taglines ship at once — no single positioning line
findings.evidence: Verbatim from homepage.html: <title>Build & Scale Your Software With Confidence | Sentrize</title>; H1 = "We build the software and cloud platforms your business runs on."; og:title / twitter:title = "Sentrize | Engineering That Scales Your Business". That is three distinct value props (confidence / platforms-you-run-on / scaling-your-business) on one page. By contrast each reference firm commits to ONE line: Netguru = "AI-native commerce"; STX Next = "AI-First, Outcome-driven Technology Partner. Built on Python Excellence"; Thoughtworks leads with a single modernization-powered-by-AI narrative.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Three taglines is the classic tell of AI-generated / unedited copy — each field was filled independently and never reconciled. A real firm has a positioning statement it repeats everywhere (SERP title, social share, hero) so the brand compounds. Divergent taglines read as a template with placeholder text nobody owned.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE positioning line and use it verbatim in <title>, og:title, twitter:title, and the H1. Recommend anchoring on a concrete niche the way STX Next does with Python — e.g. 'Sentrize — the engineering partner that ships and operates your cloud software.' Then make every meta tag match it word-for-word.

findings.title: Round-number vanity stats with no verifiable backing
findings.evidence: meta description and the homepage stat tiles claim "120+ engineers, 500+ projects, and 99.99% SLA-backed uptime across 40+ countries" plus tiles reading "500+ / Proven delivery", "99.99% / Enterprise reliability", "Real-time / Full visibility", "100% / code ownership". All are unsourced round numbers. STX Next presents comparable claims (500+ team members, 21 years on market, 1000+ projects, 20+ years Python) but ties them to a verifiable 2008 founding, named clients (Canon, Decathlon, Mastercard, GSK) and specific case-study numbers like 'reducing unplanned downtime by 20%'.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Every number ends in a suspiciously clean +0 (500+, 120+, 40+) and the 99.99% uptime is an infrastructure-SLA claim a project-based dev shop cannot credibly make company-wide. AI-generated marketing gravitates to these exact placeholder figures. With no named client or verifiable proof beneath them, they read as invented.
findings.recommendation: Either substantiate or replace. Keep only numbers you can defend and tie each to proof: e.g. '11 years in operation (founded 2015)' instead of vague scale, and replace '99.99% SLA-backed uptime' with the specific product/SLA it applies to. Attach at least one named client logo or case study to each stat tile so the number is corroborated, the way STX Next pairs its counters with the Man Group / Linde stories.

findings.title: Real recognizable client logos, but case studies name no clients — a credibility contradiction
findings.evidence: Homepage logo strip uses real brand marks: alt="Cisco", "Bitdefender", "Viewsonic", "EaseUS", "Mondly", "123 FormBuilder" (files cisco-white.png, bitdefenderr-white.png [note the misspelled double-r], viewsonic-white.png, etc.). Yet page-case-studies.html headlines are all anonymized: "Re-Architecting a Payments Core for Linear Scale", "Lift, Re-Platform, and Optimize for a Healthcare Data Provider", "Multi-Tenant Re-Platform for a B2B Collaboration Product", "FinOps and Reliability Engineering for a Consumer Streaming App". Thoughtworks and Netguru do the reverse: named client + named story + metric (Netguru: 'IKEA / OLX / Żabka' with '40% growth in international & GCC orders', '21% higher conversion').
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Showing Cisco/Bitdefender logos as if they are clients, while no case study can name a single company, is internally inconsistent — a reviewer immediately asks 'if Cisco is a client, where is the Cisco story?' The mismatch reads as borrowed logos plus fabricated-sounding generic case studies. The double-r typo in bitdefenderr-white.png reinforces that the logos were grabbed hastily.
findings.recommendation: Only display a logo if you can back it with a named, linkable case study (get written permission per client). Convert the anonymized studies into named ones with a client quote, a role-attributed name, and 2-3 real metrics each (following the Netguru pattern). If a client won't be named, remove its logo from the 'trusted by' strip rather than implying a relationship you can't evidence. Fix the bitdefenderr filename regardless.

findings.title: 'Our Team' page uses stock Unsplash portraits for named executives — reused across the site
findings.evidence: page-team.html pairs named people ("Sofia Marchetti", "Rahul Desai", "Klara Novak", "Benjamin Asante", "Yuki Tanaka", "Olivia Brandt") with Unsplash stock headshots: photo IDs 1500648767791, 1494790108377 (the famously overused 'smiling brunette' stock portrait), 1507003211169, 1438761681033, 1472099645785. The SAME photo IDs appear elsewhere on the site (each ID resolves twice in page-team.html and also across other pages). Thoughtworks, Netguru and STX Next all use genuine team/office photography of real, findable employees.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Named leaders backed by recognizable free stock faces is the single most trust-destroying signal a services firm can send — anyone who reverse-image-searches one headshot finds it on a hundred other sites. Reusing the same stock IDs in multiple places proves the 'team' is decorative filler, a hallmark of AI-assembled placeholder content.
findings.recommendation: Replace every headshot with real photographs of real Sentrize people (a consistent studio shoot: same background, lighting, crop). If specific individuals can't be shown yet, remove the named-team grid entirely and show an office/culture photo instead — an empty-but-honest team section beats fabricated bios. Never pair a real name with a stock face.

findings.title: Palette indiscipline — teal/cyan brand color polluted by three stray blues
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json backgrounds include the intended teal accents #0891b2 (x3) and #22d3ee (x3) but ALSO three unrelated blues used as accents/CTAs: #0a84ff (the iOS system blue, on the 'Skip to content' button), #004aef, and text color #3474ff. So four different accent hues compete. The stylesheet is even named theme-teal.css, implying teal is the brand, yet #0a84ff/#004aef/#3474ff leak through from the lifted template. Reference firms hold to one accent: Netguru = one green on dark, STX Next = a single teal-to-blue gradient, Thoughtworks = mostly neutral with one brand color.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Using Apple's stock #0a84ff system blue alongside a nominal teal brand shows the palette was never defined — colors were inherited from whatever template fragments were pasted in. Disciplined palettes are a baseline enterprise signal; four accents reads as unfinished.
findings.recommendation: Define one accent and one hover/active shade as CSS custom properties (e.g. --accent:#0891b2; --accent-strong:#0369a1) and replace every occurrence of #0a84ff, #004aef, and #3474ff with them. Delete unused brand blues from theme-teal.css. Keep the neutral scale you already have (#ffffff, #0b0b0f/#000, grays #5b5b6b/#9a9aad) and cap total non-neutral hues at two.

findings.title: Hero uses generic Unsplash stock photography instead of product/work imagery
findings.evidence: The hero and multiple sections pull Unsplash stock via images.unsplash.com (photo IDs 1461749280684, 1498050108023 'laptop code on desk', 1451187580459, 1460925895917, 1512941937669, etc.). The desktop hero shows a generic 'woman at laptop' stock scene beside a fake video-play thumbnail (homepage-img-video.png). Credible firms show their actual product UI, real client work, or real people: STX Next and Netguru lead with product mockups and named case-study visuals, not desk stock.
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Interchangeable corporate-desk stock photography is the visual equivalent of placeholder text — it says nothing specific about Sentrize's work and appears on thousands of other sites. Combined with the stock team photos, it makes the whole site feel populated by assets rather than evidence.
findings.recommendation: Replace hero and section imagery with screenshots of software Sentrize has actually shipped (dashboards, architecture diagrams, deployment pipelines) or a short real product demo behind the play button. If real work can't be shown for NDA reasons, use abstract branded illustration/3D that is clearly bespoke, not photo-1498050108023 which is one of the most-used stock code photos on the web.

findings.title: Anemic 300-weight headings on a payments-terminal font undercut authority
findings.evidence: design-tokens.json headings: H1 'We build the software...' is 52px at font-weight 300 (Light); every H2 ('An engineering partner built to scale...', 'Why teams keep building with us') is 44px weight 300; H3 32px weight 300. The face is Verifone-Sans, a display font tuned for a payments brand, not a software consultancy. Reference firms use bold, single-concept headline hierarchy (Netguru 'Bold headline hierarchy using large, single-concept statements'; STX Next hierarchical H1/H2 with punchy <12-word subheads).
findings.why_it_reads_amateur_or_ai: Uniform 300-weight at large sizes produces a thin, low-contrast, generic-startup look and removes hierarchy — everything whispers. Paired with a borrowed payments font, the typography carries none of Sentrize's own voice. It reads as default template settings left untouched.
findings.recommendation: After swapping Verifone-Sans for a licensed self-hosted face, set headline weight to 600-700 for H1/H2 to establish authority, keep body at 400, and build a real type scale (e.g. H1 48-56px/700, H2 32-36px/600, H3 22-24px/600, body 16-18px/400). Tighten letter-spacing slightly on large headings. Reserve light weights (300) for large decorative numerals only, not core headlines.

workflowProgress.label: color-palette
workflowProgress.promptPreview:
